Proposals for adapting corporate strategies to Generation Z peculiarities: global companies operating in the CIS region 65 Conclusion 72 References 75 Appendix 82 Introduction Theories of generations underline the idea that people born around the same time witness  similar  historical events, social movements and trends, what makes them develop similar patterns of behavior, values and preferences.  The initial theory was formulated by Karl Mannheim in the early 20 th  century, and has then been referred to in various studies, including one of  William Strauss and Neil Howe in 1991. Strauss and Howe  analyzed Mannheim’s research, and proposed their own  theory on generations ,  while starting a consulting firm focused on helping businesses adapt to different generations of stakeholders.   Th eir  theory  and research  laid the foundation for contemporary perception of generations as  a factor influencing organizations, and they majorly influenced the ways businesses perceived their stakeholders  from different generations.  The majority of studies on generational identity and intergenerational differences were conducted in the English-speaking environment, and the findings were often limited by geographical and socio-cultural contexts. However, as globalization , connectivity  and a ccess   to the  technologies and the Internet soared, ideas on  intergenerational differences  and generational identity become more relevant  to companies on international and various local markets.  As the first representatives of Generation Z (born 1997-2012) are currently becoming solvent consumers and have just entered or are currently entering the labor markets, it is important for companies to identify distinctive characteristics of this generation and adapt their strategies and business processes to them. The relevance of the paper is based on the fact   that  since  the oldest representatives of Generation Z have just graduated from universities, there has not yet been much research on this generation from the perspective of  them being stakeholders for businesses .  The reason for considering the generation as consumers and employees  is the fact that these are the ways zoomers, as businesses’ stakeholders, have the most impact on them.  The consideration of  The theory of generations   by companies in the CIS region has started relatively recently, and is mostly due to most international businesses being from the regions where the theory had been acknowledged long ago, to globalization and to increased global connectivity.  The research gap  in the area is identified as  t he impact of Generation Z  specificities  in the CIS markets on international companies' strategic management .  The goal of this paper  is to develop strategic proposals for international companies, which are willing to adapt to Generation Z  peculiarities  i n the CIS  market .  The objectives are: Identify generations concept and describe theories of generations; Consider the influence of  the theories of generations  on organizations’ strategic management; Analyze previous generational shifts and their impact on   businesses ;  Analyze  global trends related to  Generation Z  and  their  impact on the markets; Analyze peculiarities of Generation Z in t he  CIS  countries from the perspective of their influence on the markets ;  Conduct  analysis on how companies have adapted  to generations shifts  from the  historical perspective and how they  are currently adapting  to Generation Z peculiarities; Fo rmulate  proposals f or  international  companies to  help them  successfully adapt to peculiarities of Generation Z   in the CIS market. The  object of this research is p eculiarities  of Generation Z as  businesses’ stakeholders , and the subject is impact of  those  peculiarities on companies’ corporate strategies.  The first part of the thesis provides an overview of the conceptual framework related to the topic of  the  theory of generations and intergenerational differences and explains the methodology used in this paper. This lays the foundation for further analysis of Generation Z distinctive characteristics in their patterns of behavior  as stakeholders for businesses, specifically as consumers, job seekers and employees for the above-mentioned reason .   Primary data, in a form of a questionnaire for representatives of Generation Z and interviews with senior marketing and HR managers  of international companies present globally and in the countries of the CIS , and secondary data, in a form of an analysis of literature and studies in the area will be discussed in this section. The third part of the paper provides a historical perspective on how organizations were adapting to generational shifts in the past and an overview of how they are currently adapting to Generation Z.  The findings of obtained primary and secondary data are used for formulation of pr oposals  in the last part of the paper.  The se  p roposals for adaption of companies’ strategies to Generation Z in the CIS region are based on businesses’ experience globally and on peculiarities  of zoomers in CIS countries.  The methodology includes secondary data collected from studies of various scholars and research centers,  a s  there is   an overview of different publications, both the initial  fundamental ones and publications from contemporary reliable sources. The  primary data  is obtained  from interviews with managers of international companies present in the CIS countries and a survey among Generation Z representatives in the same region.  The interviews were conducted with  senior  marketing and HR  managers  of companies from the industries of FMCG, apparel, QSR, telecommunication, and coworking space.  The paper is structured in the way to develop strategic proposals for companies by first analyzing generational shifts’ impacts on markets, peculiarities of Generation Z from the perspective of global trends and local CIS specificities, and the ways companies have adapted to various generations in the past and are adapting to Generation Z currently.  CHAPTER 1.  Theory of genetations and its  role in  management of organizations This  chapter  provides an overview of terminology, key concepts and most influential theories on the topic of generational identity and intergenerational differences. Addressing historical background, fundamental generational theories and terminology used for labeling contemporary generations is necessary for further analysis of how organizations and their approaches to strategic management get influenced by generational changes. The conceptual framework overview aims at analyzing how the influence of socio-historical environment influences generations, what criteria have already been used for comparing generations currently present in the society, and what research gap and research questions can be addressed in this paper. Along with the overview of key terminology and concepts, the findings include criteria for analyzing generations, as well as justification for considering a particular  theory on generations  and analysis of this theory’s impact on organizations. 1.1 .   Major approaches to generations ’  definition and theories of generations The concept of generations underlines the idea that people born around the same time build up a certain uniqueness that distinguishes them from people born in other generational cohorts .  Generation definition is elusive, so scholars in different disciplines have been attempting to define this phenomenon.  S tudies on generations have had a distinguished place in social sciences for a long time,  as  scholars have been searching for unique distinctive generational characteristics for the last few decades.  For example, K upperschmidt (2000) defined generation as “an identifiable group that shares birth years, age location, and significant life events at critical development stage” ,  and believed that people who encounter similar significant events   in young adulthood , produce a common/shared memory of them which later has an effect on  their  behavior , preferences, values and interests .                The first systematic and fully developed research on generational cohorts was published by Karl Mannheim in his 1928 essay called “The Problem of Generations”,  where he  formulated  his  Theory of Generations , which later became fundamental to all subsequent stud ies   related to  generations  in general and generations  as  a   concept influencing management of businesses .  The essay  is  mostly focused on the idea that  people  who are born around the same time tend to have  “certain similar modes of behavior, feeling and thought” .  According to Mannheim’s theory, this happens due to the fact that people are significantly influenced by the socio-historical environment they belonged to when they were young, while the biggest influence is caused by major historical events  that people  witnessed . This shared experience leads to people forming certain social cohorts, which were called “generations” in the essay, what later sparked a lot of debate on whether the terminology was correct.  Moreover, Mannheim pointed out the fact that generations identified by him are internally stratified by their geographical, cultural and social background, as well as by the ways in which they react to particular situations, as this causes the emergence of distinguishing units within generations.  As Mannheim stressed the key role of shared experience and knowledge,  he   believed that birth   year itself is not  sufficient  enough to put a person into a certain generation, and the bigger role is played by historical events  one  experienced or actively participated in.  However,  studies and theories ,  which emerged after Mannheim’s  Theory of Generations ,   examine the theory of people   born around the same time hav ing  similar characteristics and patterns of behavior,  as  the ir  generational identity  is mostly dependent on people’s age and year of birth rather than only on socioeconomic background. Because of th e  likelihood of generations  having  distinguished units within them ,  many researchers questioned  reliability of Mannheim’s choice of terminology, and specifically  the usage of the word “generation” in  his  essay. For example, Glenn (1977) and Rosow (1978) stated that since Mannheim talks about people who experience the same significant events over similar time periods, the words “cohort” or “generational cohort” should be used.  D espite dissent of some scholars, “The Problem of Generations” became the seminal publication that caused changes in terminology and introduced generations from a perspective  of their impact on the spheres outside of social sciences .  Written in 1928 and then translated into English in 1952, Mannheim’s essay is  mostly  focused on the Western youth and the ways it was influenced by  different  historical events.  However, it had a crucial role for any research on generations and significantly influenced the  perception of different generations. As Mannheim focused on the concept of shared knowledge and formulated his theory in the beginning of the 20 th  century, he didn’t have an opportunity to study the differences between generations, which are now present in the society.  Therefore, Mannheim’s research provided the base for the more recent studies on generations, but his  Theory of Generations   is not directly linked to the specificities of generations analyzed later in this thesis  due to the restrictions of the concept, which are geographical (Mannheim only considered developed Western countries) and time-related, as the paper was written before internet was invented, so the society which Mannheim analyzed in his essay is extremely different from what will be analyzed in this thesis.  T heory on generational developmen t   that is currently addressed as the most influential,  was posed by William Strauss and Neil Howe in 199 1 , and is addressed as  The Strauss-Howe Generational Theory  or  The Fourth Turning Theory .  The research conducted by Strauss and Howe laid the foundation for current perception of generations and their distinguishing features  from the perspective of strategic management.  The authors focused on differences between generations and came up with a theory that generational development is cyclical and it takes approximately twenty years for one generation to switch the previous one. Unlike Mannheim, Strauss and Howe  consider  the group at large and not units within the same generation , what might have caused more generalized perception of the generations ; moreover,  The Fourth Turning Theory   does not underestimate the importance of the birth year and implies that people have distinctive characteristics according to the years they were born in . In their original formulation of the theory Howe and Strauss  believed that the birth   year is important because if people encounter similar key historical events or inventions at the same age, they are more likely to develop the same distinctive characteristics, and then start belonging to the same social generation or cohort . They based their perception and definition of the concept of generations on many scholars,  from ancient writers  Greek historic Polybius and Arab scholar Ibn Khaldun  to relatively  contemporary  Karl Mannheim . Strauss-Howe’s  theory on generations  was  published  in 1991 in their book called  Generations , and  since then the  works of Strauss and Howe  have been extremely influential in the study of  the impact of generational identity on the way businesses are managed .  Moreover, t he  way the  authors  thought of generations having a span of 20 years and their  chosen  labels/names of generations  are  still widely used today.  Moreover, staring from 1991 up to the mid-2000s both William Strauss and Neil Howe actively studied differences between generations   from business perspective , while managing a  consulting  firm specified on helping  companies  benefit  from adapting their strategic management to  various distinctive features of generations . The  generational  labels,  proposed by  Strauss and Howe and their descriptions of generations are now  widely common worldwide , and  the  Strauss-Howe Generational Theory   is still widely discussed and referenced, but now mostly with a focus on the way the authors distinguished generations and identified differences between them.  The generational labels, proposed by William Strauss and Neil Howe, became vernacular in any discussion about generations, and they include Baby boomers (born 1946-1964), Generation X (born 1965-1980), Generation Y/Millenials (born 1981-1996) and Generation Z/Zoomers (born 1997-2012).  The generation of  b aby boomers is usually defined as the people born in the post-World War II period. As the last generation which matured before the boundaries became weaker due to a global online web, generation’s dates, demographic and cultural contexts may wary in different countries . Generation  X , which follows the baby boomers and precedes  the millennials, is often identified by researchers and mass media as the people who were born somewhere from the mid-1960s to the late 1970s or early 1980s . Millenials have their birth dates set up as the beginning of 1980s to the mid-1990s or early 2000s, with the range 1981-1996 being the most commonly accepted   in discussions and publications .  The last generation discussed by Strauss and Howe and the one that is going to be considered in this thesis is addressed as Generation Z or Zoomers. There are currently no fixed dates for this generation, but most sources use mid-1990s as the starting point for Generation Z and early 2010s as the ending birth years.  The research centers and media, which cite this range as the relevant one, include The Pew Research Center , McKinsey&Company , the BBC , Deloitte , The New York Times  and many others.  The last identified generation is called Generation Alpha and it hasn’t been yet analyzed much due to the fact that the widely accepted range for this generation is early 2010s – mid-2020s, and  there is yet no sufficient base for analyzing the representatives due to their young age . The table, developed by the Pew Research Center to simplify understanding of generations’ dates is presented below: Generational labels   as they were introduced by Strauss and Howe As generational theories of Mannheim, Strauss and Howe set up the ground for further discussions , preceding research was mostly focused on identifying the differences between generations and understanding how they can be helpful in different spheres, including business, entertainment, politics, and etc. However, as factors related to differences between generations can  be complex and  might  overlap, researchers  often distinguish three effects that cause differences in behavior and features between age groups. Pew Research Center, which  specializes  in data-driven social science research, has played a significant role in understanding generational development and identity. The three separate effects, which produce differences in generations’ behavior, are listed as the following, by the specialists from the center:   the life cycle/age effect, the period effect and the cohort effect .  Understanding the concepts behind the effects is important as they play a key role in causing intergenerational differences . The first life cycle or age effect implies that some differences between generations might be due to the differences in people’s age, what means that distinguishing features between younger and older people might be caused by their positions in the life cycle. For example, if we compare millennials and Generation X, the latter might seem to be less into politics. According  to the life cycle effect, this is due to the fact that as people get older, more of them get informed about politics and vote. Millenials are less engaged in politics, but same was true for Generation X or baby boomers when they were younger.   Period effect is another process considered when intergenerational differences are discussed. Period effect implies that major events and  circumstances , such as different social movements,  financial crises, technological or scientific backgrounds and etc. have a significant influence on people, and different perception of such circumstances might affect the way generations develop distinguishing characteristics.  Finally, the third process is addressed as the cohort effect. When this effect is at play, differences between generations are explained  as  the consequence s  of unique historical circumstances  faced by members of generational cohorts. In some cases, it might be of a period effect that one generation experienced, but the rests did not; this can refer to wars experienced by older generations . This effect coincides with the theory of Karl Mannheim, with an exception that contemporary researchers consider generations at large, while Mannheim focused on relatively small units of people.  Defining and understanding   the concepts of these effects  is the key to comprehending  both “classic” and recent literature on the topic of intergenerational difference.   1.2 .   I nfluence   of  the  T heory  of generations  on strategic management of organizations: research gap and research questions   In this part, role of generations theory in management, the importance and relevance of analyzing how organizations can adapt their corporate strategies and business processes to the major peculiarities of Generation Z and  an identified research gap are discussed.   As it was  mentioned  earlier, the concept of generations is based on the idea that people born around the same time experience similar major historical events, social movements and trends, what makes them develop alike patterns of behavior, values and preferences. It implies the idea that people who belong to  the same  generation tend to develop a certain generational identity, based on shared knowledge and experience. Thus, representatives of different generations are likely to have distinctive characteristics in major aspects of their lives, including the way the consume and work.  One of the fundamental  researches  on generational identity was conducted by  Kupperschmidt (2000), who s tud ied  generations and the way they behave  as employees and consumers .   The findings included the fact  that  generations as social construct  develop a certain generational identity or personality, that influences people’s feelings regarding authority and organizations, what their needs at work are and what they do to satisfy those needs .  Different needs, expectations and patterns of behavior are caused by  different generations having distinguished values and preferences, and this affects not only the way people perceive their colleagues and responsibilities at work, but also the way they consume. As it was mentioned earlier, generations appear within a 20-years span, and the fact that people of different generations were growing up witnessing and encountering different things, majorly affects them as consumers and employees. As globalization soars, generational shifts are expected to play a crucial role in influencing people’s patterns of behavior, so it is extremely important for businesses to acknowledge the differences among generations and adapt their strategies, practices and business-processes to  new waves of consumers and employees. Generations theory allows organizations to understand when the generational shifts occur and what are the aspects in which new consumers and employees can differ from their predecessors.  Conducted  analysis of literature and studies  on generational identity and intergenerational differences showed that most of the research in this area is focused on millenials. Generation Z,  being born approximately between 1997 and 2012, have just recently entered the workforce and become solvent consumers, so they have not been analyzed much from the perspective of implications for businesses.  Moreover, the theories of generations were proposed by Western scholars, and have been discussed mostly with the focus on representatives of generations from the United States and Europe. The reason for that was the fact that generational theories were originally based on historical events and social movements in the West, and, therefore, concepts of “boomers” or “baby-boomers” could not apply to countries, which did not encounter events behind the reasons of why the generations were formed and perceived that way. However, the world has become more globalized, and accessible internet has significantly influenced the way people from different countries and cultures perceive information, communicate, react to historical events and social movements, and exchange opinions. Thus, it can be stated that the generation that grow up being the first digital natives, mature and have meaningful life experiences in relation to not only their country or culture, but also on the global boundaryless online area.  This became especially evident after the global lockdown dur to the pandemic occurred, as young people all over the world  started exchanging their experiences and ideas on more platforms, what erased the boundaries and showed how similar are the representatives of the new generation from different countries. However, despite zoomers living in and consuming information in the boundaryless age of the internet, cultural and socioeconomic background still play a huge role in how people perceive the world and behave, so this paper discusses global trends related to zoomers from the perspective of business, while also emphasizing local peculiarities and specificities common for Generation Z representatives from the CIS countries.  The relevance of the paper  is based on the fact that the oldest representatives of Generation Z are 22-24 years in 2021, they have just recently become solvent consumers and are currently joining the workforce, so there has not yet been much research on this generation from the perspective of  them as consumers and employees. However, considering the importance of Generation Z peculiarities for businesses, zoomers start to become many organizations’ stakeholders, and as stakeholders they play the biggest role and have the most impact on companies specifically as consumers and potential or current employees.  Therefore,  the research gap in the area  is identified as the correlation between Generation Z characteristics and their interpretation and usage  from the perspective of strategic management of global companies working in the CIS region .  In order to fulfill the  research  gap, the following  research questions  are formulated: Question 1:  What  are the major  peculiarities of Generation Z  and how do they  affect  representatives’  patterns of behavior  in areas, which influence businesses’ strategic management ?  Question 2:  What are companies currently doing to  adapt their strategies and business processes t o Generation Z? Question 3:   What should  businesses  do to  improve their corporate and business strategies by considering the features of Generation Z  in the CIS region ? To answer these questions, various types of data are going to be collected and analyzed further in the paper with a goal of setting a ground for formulation of practical implications designed to help companies adapt to Generation Z as their consumers and employees.  The relevant research methodology is discussed in the next section. 1.3 .  Research methodology T his  paper  is focused on  how  companies can improve their strategies by  considering  distinctive features  of Generation Z,  and  the methodology is designed in a way that allow s  to have the  earlier mentioned research  questions  answered as the  study  is completed .  To answer the questions, both primary and secondary data  has been  collected, and this part describes the data needed to formulate  strategic proposals for companies  and   justifies  he choice of instruments applied to collect that data.  As  the first research question is  focused  on characteristics of Generation Z as consumers and employees , se condary data on distinctive  features  of this generation has  been  collected.  According to the reviewed literature, generations are often studied from the viewpoint of comparing generation that is being analyzed to the previous one. This can be explained by the fact that practical implications for businesses can be derived from  specific  differences between new generation and its predecessors according to specific criteria for comparison and analysis.  Not all features of generations are different, some generations – especially if they are close in terms of the time span, like millennials and Generation Z ,  have similarities, and this is another reason why it is important to set up criteria for analyzing generations, as this allow s  to identify both similarities and differen ces , as they can be used by businesses.  Therefore, as it is important to understand what patterns of behavior,  preferences, values and just general characteristics are common for representatives of Generation Z, the analysis of research that was focused on deriving and describing those features will be held later in the first part of Chapter 2. The  secondary data,  discussed in that part,  is  related to description of Generation Z distinctive features in comparison to their predecessors, and analysis of those features from the viewpoint of how they affect the behavior of zoomers as   key  stakeholders for businesses in the CIS region .  The aspects,  which  have been  searched for in secondary sources, include key differences between Generation Z and millennials in how they behave as consumers and employees, their values, preferences, priorities when choosing an employer, and factors influencing their decision-making processes on consumer and labor markets.  The findings from secondary data are related to the ways in which Generation Z is different from the previous generation of millennials and  to the aspects in which these peculiarities affect  businesses’ strategic management .  The sources of secondary data include various publications, different forms of research, interviews and videos on the topic.  As the first wave of zoomers  started entering the consumer and labor markets (and, therefore, becoming businesses’ stakeholders)  just a couple of years ago, most research on  the topic is focused on older generations, and only the most recent publications were able to analyze Generation Z. Therefore, as there is a research gap connected to how  organizations can and should adapt to the peculiarities of Generation Z as their key stakeholders , it is crucial to get the firsthand information from  zoomers  themselves.  For that ,  a  survey  for young adults of the age of 18-24 years w as  distributed.  The questionnaire was filled by 2 22  representatives of Generation Z aged between 18-24 years old from  28  different cities  in 7  countries, including Russia, Kazakhstan, Ukraine, Kyrgyzstan, Armenia, Belarus, and Tajikistan .   The questionnaire consists of three sections with questions:   The first part  is focused on respondents’ background, including their age, city of residence, attended university (if any), and their current year of studying. The goal of this section is to gather information that will later help to identify correlation between any of the factors, as people’s patterns of behavior as employees and consumer behavior might be shaped according to their country of residence or academic experience. Aside from that, the first section also contained a general question on what a respondent plans to achieve before the age of thirty – this question is not directly related to consumer or labor markets, but the idea behind it is that the things that one wants to achieve before a certain age show their priorities and values, which are likely to affect decision-making processes when making a purchase or looking for an employer.    The distribution of respondents according to their year of  studies is illustrated below: Characteristics of  the questionnaire  respondents Despite the fact that zoomers are now aged within the range of 9 to 24 years, only those representatives who are likely to have control over their purchases and have work experience or are likely to acquire it in the nearest future are being surveyed.  The  justification for   surveying only those  representatives of Generation Z  who are at least 18 years is that at this age  most  people get enrolled into universities and start to think about their future careers more consciously, so they  are more likely to provide qualitative data related to their behavior as consumers, job seekers or  employees.   The sample is designed to consist of people born in 1996-2003,  and among the 222 respondents, the distribution in terms of representatives’ cities and attended universities was the following: Bishkek, Kyrgyzstan – 65 respondents  Saint Petersburg, Russia – 47 respondents   Moscow, Russia – 14 respondents  Kazan, Russia – 13 respondents Almaty, Kazakhstan – 12 respondents  Kyiv, Ukraine – 10 respondents Minsk, Belarus – 9 respondents  Yerevan, Armenia – 7 respondents  Dushanbe, Tajikistan – 5 respondents For assuring that the collected information is valid to be  considered for developing practic al implication s for MNCs working in the CIS region,  the results of the survey  were  analyzed and  compared  to the secondary data, which describes issues similar to the ones pointed out in the  questionnaire results analysis .   Moreover, as some surveys on Generation Z values and patterns of behavior  as stakeholders  have  taken place worldwide , the results of the questionnaire conducted specifically for this research and the results from earlier surveys among zoomers  have been  compared  for identifying key specificities for generation representatives from the CIS countries .  When crucial differences between global trends for Generation Z as businesses’ stakeholders and results from post-soviet countries respondents were spotted , possible reasons for that  were considered and  discussed, and those include d  cultural specificities, influence of recent major historical events or the fact that the respondents were of a different age, and, despite belonging to the same generation, people are likely to have different patterns of behavior depending on their  position in the life cycle . This peculiarity was discussed earlier as a part of conceptual base, and it is called the  age effect.  The second part of the questionnaire  is aimed at gathering information  on factors influencing the  behavior patterns of  Generation Z representatives  as job seekers and employees , as this is the perspective from which zoomers, as stakeholders, can have major influence on organizations.    The questions were designed in a way that their answers could later help to adapt businesses’ strategic human resources management from different perspectives, including recruitment, planning, training and development, talent management, and corporate culture.  The respondents from the sample of 18-24 years old zoomers are expected to help identify  factors that are most crucial when they are looking for a job. This is a very common  question for primary data collection on generations and their patterns of behavior, and it is expected to help companies adjust their employer value proposition to meet the needs and expectations of new job seekers entering the market. Another question is formulated to find out the reasons, which are most likely to make Generation Z representatives quit the job, and while the first question was aimed at later helping companies to recruit zoomers, the goal of the second one is to collect information that will help make Generation Z employees stay.  Moreover, in order to understand what might influence a general perception of organizations by zoomers, the question on what should be the ultimate goal for all organizations is going to be asked. This question is aimed at deriving information on respondents’ insights regarding what activities and features are important for businesses.  The next  question  is asked to confirm or reject the fact that people who belong to Generation Z prefer to work on tasks by themselves, and tend to dislike group projects or any activities that require team work.  Lastly, one more question of this section is aimed at identifying if the  common opinion  that representatives of Generation Z ,  unlike millennials ,  need constant guidance and support from the supervisors  is true.  Collecting responses on th e above-described   issue s  is  now crucially important  because the results  can  affect companies’ practices and approaches towards management of teams and  supervision of their employees who belong to the Generation Z .  Finally, the third section of the questionnaire  is aimed at  collecting  information  that can be used for adapting companies’ strategies on consumer markets to representatives of Generation Z.  The choice of the market is justified by the fact that one of the two main ways in which zoomers as stakeholders affect businesses is by being their consumers.   The questions  in the first part of the section  are about things that respondents consider inappropriate for businesses’ advertising campaigns,  factors and characteristics they pay most attention to when choosing fast moving consumer goods and  preferred goods. The choice of these particular types of goods is due to the fact that they differ in terms of price segments, time and effort necessary to make  a   purchasing  decision, and durability. Therefore,  since these catego ries are different in terms of so many aspects,  it is expected that asking questions about these two kinds of products will allow to understand general patterns in decision-making processes when zoomers are making a purchase.   The  answer   options includ e  various aspects, from product’s characteristics, such as quality, durability and price, to company’s brand image peculiarities and advertising campaigns. Also, the third section include s  questions , which are likely to derive information on zoomers’ consumer behavior, and the issues such as brand loyalty, preference for material or non-material goods and sustainable consumption are covered. The questionnaire as an instrument is chose due to the fact that it allows to collect information on Generation Z patterns of behavior and decision-making processes. Moreover, the sample consisted of 18-24 years old zoomers, which are part of the first wave that is currently entering the consumer and labor markets. Therefore, it is expected that the respondents are relatively solvent consumers and are already working or are considering their job prospects, so they will be able to help understand zoomers as consumers, job seekers or employees.  To answer the research questions identified earlier, it  was  also necessary to find out how companies have been adapting to various generations in the past and how they are currently shaping their strategies to make them more appealing to representatives of Generation Z.  For that,  secondary data from the literature and the media  has been  collected, and for primary data, interviews with  representatives of various  international companies  present in the CIS region ha ve  been  conducted.  The companies, from which interviewed senior HR and marketing managers were, are Unilever, Levi’s, Beeline, Ololo, and KFC.  These interviews, along with the collected secondary data, are aimed at understanding how  global  companies  acting in post-soviet countries perceive the theories of generations, how Generation Z peculiarities affect organizations’ strategic management and how companies  are  currently  adjusting their strategies  and processes  to the  specificities  of Generation Z, and what are the advantages and risks of doing so.  T he  criteria for choosing industries  for interviews with the companies  are  provided below :  International presence; Presence in the  CIS  market; Industry variability; Industries that include products of traditional demand and the newest products (appeared after the 3-4 th  industrial revolutions); Industries that produce tangible and intangible goods. The choice of criteria is based on a necessity to have companies that are present on both  CIS  and the global market, that produce different tangible or intangible goods, and that include some of the goods which appeared after the 3-4 th  industrial revolutions.  According to the criteria, respondents from the industries of FMCG, apparel,  telecommunication,  QSR,  and  coworking space industry  are expected to  be able to provide data on companies’ adaptation to Generation Z in industries, which are very different, so it will be possible to have an overview of adaptation in general, for various businesses, without one particular industry or type of products.   Choosing MNCs from the above-mentioned industries allows to meet all the identified criteria.  In total,  7  interviews  have been  conducted with senior managers  from the industries mentioned above ,  and  insights from  them  were used for formulating the overall perception of Generation Z by businesses in the CIS region and for developing strategic proposals for more effective adaptation to the peculiarities of zoomers.   The questions for the interviews  with HR managers are focused on two main  areas,  the first is about the ways specialists perceive Generation Z and differences they have noticed in zoomers in comparison to the previous generations, and the second one is about the ways companies adapt their HR practices to Generation Z that is currently entering the workforce.  The first part is aimed at getting results that help to understand distinctive features of employees belonging to Generation  Z. To find out more about their patterns of behavior, questions about zoomers’ values, ability to work in teams, attitude towards different types of remuneration  were  asked. Moreover, a comparison of millennials and zoomers  has been   addressed , since Generation Y is the most recent generation to join the workforce before zoomers, and  understanding of similarities and differences between these two generations  was  important when practical implications for adaptation of strategies  were  developed.  A part of the interview  is  dedicated to companies’ employer brand, specifically the ways it  changed before, how it is being adapted to the Generation  Z  right now, and what are the predictions for it in the future. Understanding how companies’ employer value proposition is being adapted to zoomers (if it is) is important because it allow s   to get insights on how businesses perceive Generation Z, what  later helps  to develop practical implications.  Also,  as some aspects of employer brand are in constant adjustment, it is also significant to find out if companies from different industries adapt their employer value proposition to social movements or significant social issues, such as diversity, important of mental health and mental issues awareness, inclusivity, equality, and etc. Understanding what social issues are now considered by companies is help s to  understand zoomers  as stakeholders  better, as employer value proposition changes according to the peculiarities and needs of potential job seekers.  Moreover, questions about companies’  zoomer  employees  were  asked: it is important to  unde rstand  HR managers’ opinion on whether some characteristics which are believed to be held by Generation Z are common for zoomers in their companies.  Many of the issues that  have been  addressed are findings from the previous research on Generation Z; for example, many studies point out that more than 2/3 of zoomers are interested in or planning to start their own business, and, since this majorly influences their loyalty towards current employers, it is important to either confirm or reject this fact according to the interviews and the questionnaire.  The list of the questions for the interviews can be found in Appendix  B  and Appendix C . The main goals of conducting interviews with HR managers is to get insights on distinctive features of Generation Z employees, find out how companies adapt their practices to zoomers if they do, and collect information on how their employer value proposition and HR practices are expected to change because of Generation Z in the nearest future.  The second set of the interviews is going to be conducted with  either marketing managers or brand owners of companies from the industries discussed earlier. The goal of these interviews is to gather information on how companies adapt their presence on consumer markets according  to the peculiarities of Generation Z.  The questions are aimed to collect information about the ways companies view zoomers’ distinctive characteristic and adapt their marketing strategies to them.  To be able to develop practical implications for businesses, it is vital to understand what companies are already doing to meet the needs of newly solvent consumers. The questions are designed in a way to understand how (if) socially important topics, such as inclusivity or equality, that are widely discussed among zoomers influence companies’ value proposition, advertising campaigns and methods of translating their messages to the consumers. Moreover, for further implications, it is necessary to understand how companies communicate with their consumers or promote their products, and if the channels or ways of communicating are different depending on the generation or an age of the consumer. Again, the comparison between millennials and zoomers  was  addressed, as these two generations are the last ones to join the consumer and labor markets, so it is important to understand how companies changed for millennials to know what should or should not be adapted to  Generation Z. Other issues, such as sustainable development, digital nativity and changes of positioning and promotion of companies from different industries  are  be discussed, and the pool of questions is available in the Appendix  A .  The methodology of this paper is designed to help answer the research questions, which are related to the peculiarities of Generation Z  affecting businesses , ongoing adaptation of companies to this generation and practical implications that can help make businesses’ presence on consumer and labor markets more appealing to zoomers, who have just recently become solvent consumers, job seekers and employees.  T o develop these practical implications,  secondary data from the literature and the media and primary data in a form of a questionnaire among zoomers and interviews with companies from four different industries  has been  collected. The findings from both types of data  are  discussed and analyzed further in this thesis.  Chapter 2.  generational changes and thei r  impact on  the markets   2 .1 .   Historical perspective of generation al changes Despite the fact that the concept of generations has become widely known and accepted globally   relatively recently , the idea of people   belonging to different generation s  with a span of approximately twenty years is not new for  businesses in the Western part of the world . As it has been pointed out and discussed earlier,  globalization has drastically increased the role of the  theory on generations , as global companies, which perceived the concept to be legitimate, are currently implementing the same approach of dividing their stakeholders into different cohorts according to the generations they belong to.  This part is aimed at analyzing changes of generations and the ways they impacted businesses, a nd  this information will contribute to the understanding  of how this shift to Generation Z can affect organizations .  I t is important to take a historical perspective on generational shifts and their influence on strategic management and business-processes  of organizations , as this will help to  assess  the  possible  impact of Generation Z on companies and, thus, develop proposals for organizations, which are interested in building solid relationships with their Generation Z stakeholders,  specifically   with  consumers and employees, since this is the way zoomers  as stakeholders  have most influence on businesses.  As this part provides  an  overview of how changes of generations affected consumer and labor markets in the past, it is important to point out that  the trends and shifts discussed particularly here are global, but with a major consideration of stakeholders of the Western businesses, since before globalization soared, generational shifts were only considered in certain parts of the world. However,  since many of the MNCs, which are now thriving in the CIS region, origi nated in the West, they were influenced by  further described  generational shifts, so there is an evident necessity to analyze the effect of generational shifts on the markets since it will help to understand general tendencies in changes occurring  to the workplaces and the ways people consume .   L ater in the thesis the justification behind why and how generational shifts are currently influencing businesses all over the world will be provided and discussed in detail , but for now it vital to consider what has already changed because of changes of generations.  Changes of generations  affect  businesses’ strategies, practices and processes ,   when  consumer and labor markets get entered by representatives of  new  generations  with their  distinguished values and needs,  what makes   organizations adapt in order to stay competitive in  winning  over consumers and employees.  The  three  generations, currently  active ly engaged  in working and consum ing , are Generation X, Generation Y (millennials) and Generation Z (zoomers) , and the breakdown according to age is currently the following :  Gen eration  X:  born between 1965 and 1979/80 ,  currently 41-56 years old  Gen Y   or Millennials :  born between 1981 and 1994/6 ,  currently 25 - 40 years old  Gen Z: born between 1997 and 2012 ,  currently  9 - 24 years old   Generational cohorts’ labels do not belong to exact science, they rather represent a reference to people’s experience, historical events and behavioral patterns being grouped with an approximate 20 years span.  Using those generational names and labels or not, the phenomenon of new waves of consumers and employees being influenced by events and trends and thus having completely different behavioral patterns and values has been discussed for a long time.  The first generation, that was thoroughly analyzed from the perspective of its representatives being different when serving a role of business stakeholders, was Generation X, which started influencing organizations roughly in early 1980s, when they started entering consumer and labor markets.  They are drastically different from their predecessors baby-boomers, as this generation is much more digitally savvy, being influenced by the rise of personal computing and  the Internet .  Generalizing all people born between 1965 and 1980 under one label  is difficult and rather incorrect, as most representatives of Generation X, unlike millennials and zoomers, did not mature in a globalized world consuming similar content and experiencing similar trends , and this especially true for the older Gen Xers, as they are closer in their mentality to the previous generation.  T he  shift to this generation was accompanied by emergence of technologies, and it distinguishes Generation X from their predecessors . Considering rising globalization and increase in intercultural  interactions, this generation was also exposed to other cultures in a greater extent than boomers and baby-boomers, what helped them have a broader  view of the world and shaped their perception of  individuals .  This, supposedly, not only helped Gen Xers to adapt a more flexible and open-minded perception of their surroundings and the world, but also affected the way they saw brands and employers, as this generation is considered to be the pioneers of making the workplace more employee-friendly.  There is a common  conception  that representatives of  this generation  are less fear-driven compared  to baby-boomers, who had their childhood in a post-war period.  Moreover, they had less trust towards authority than their parents did, and they are known to be more skeptical about power being concentrated in government and companies. While previous generations had strong work ethic and were driven by it, Generation X are known to be more self-reliant and having a balanced, rather than driven or dedicated work ethic.  They were unimpressed by the authority, and this is especially relevant when speaking of younger representatives  of this generation , since the y are closer by their mentality and patterns of behavior to millennials rather than to baby-boomers . In business environment, the perception of employees drastically changed after Generation X has entered labor market, a nd this entailed changes in workplace too.  The concept of a traditional workplace with security from the institution, promotions based on longevity and extreme loyalty to the organization got replaced by what is addressed as new generation workplace, and it is a combination of security from within, promotions being mostly based on performance, loyalty to the team and not the organization, and an ability of challenging the authority, while granting respect after it is earned and not just expected due to a position or title .  In one of the previous researc h  done by  Andrea Healey of the ACA , key  characteristics of Generation X  were defined as the following :  Divers ity :  since this cohort was the first one to be exposed to other cultures at a large extent, they, in comparison to baby-boomers, have  different perception and attitude towards   diverse ethnicity, age, and socioeconomic status among  their co-workers and surroundings ;  Success:   Many Generation X representatives witnessed their parents and siblings get  downsiz ed at their workplaces during the 90s turmoil, which occurred in many countries including the USA and post-soviet countries .  This affected their perception of success, as Xers wanted to be more stable than their parents, and showed strong commitment towards their teams and goals, considering  socially praised  career   as an indicator of success ;  Pragmatism:   Generation X, especially their older representatives, are very pragmatic, what is evident in the ways they consume and perceive their careers. This generation, unlike their predecessors, have a big entrepreneurial spirit within it, as they are less fear-driven than baby-boomers and are more willing to try new things. Moreover, Xers are known for not questioning success as a social construct, but instead they  have relatively socially acceptable and common beliefs and goals in how education, career and family make a person more successful.  Attitudes and values:   Generation X is commonly considered to be the pioneers in questioning the role of organization in the lives, as its representatives transferred being loyal to the organization to being loyal to the team, and this was another challenge for the HR managers. Exposure to other cultures and countries changed people’s perception of self and other individuals, and representatives of Generation X are much more self-reliant than their predecessors baby-boomers.  This generation does have work ethic and loyalty, but self-reliance also let them acquire the feeling of working for themselves  a nd not for the company.   Seeing their parents dedicate their lives to workplaces and not getting much in return or even getting fired when the firms were downsizing, made Generation X not willing to only have one employer for life, and this also affected their behavior at the workplace. Representatives of this generation are known to be unimpressed by the authority and very autonomous in how they complete tasks, as they do not like micro-management and prefer to get things done by themselves .  Generation X is often referred to as the  silent  or  lost generation   because they got sandwiched between two drastically different generations – millennials and baby-boomers. They had the role of the bridge from conservative and fear-driven baby-boomers to open-minded and globalized millennials, and thus often get lost when these two extremes get compared. However, the role of Generation X should not be ignored in this transition, as the younger representatives of this generation were the pioneers in moving from living for working to being self-conscious as employees and consumers, and this drastically changed the workplace and the way employees were perceived by their companies .  Millennials, or Generation Y, were born within the years of 1980 and late 1990s , and they are the generation at which  the T heory of generations transitioned from being a Western concept to being commonly known and accepted globally.  It  is not  an  exaggeration to state that millennials reshaped the business world, since they, as businesses’ stakeholders, changed everything – including the ways companies produce,  promote, communicate with consumers and employees, and maintain their workplaces .  Millennials’  values, lifestyle choices, consuming habits and proficiency in technologies let them have a big impact on the ways businesses are managed all over the world , as corporations had to adapt to the new wave of consumers and employees.  There are many characteristics, which distinguish millennials from their prior generations, and one of the most discussed one is their prioritization of purpose. While their predecessors Generation X were mostly self-focused and relied on themselves, millennials are team-players, which need to know that they are not just working, but instead making a positive change in the society. This desire to have certain positive impact influenced not only their attitude towards employers and workplaces, but also shaped the way they consume.  With slight generalization, it is fair to state that Generation Y or millennials were the pioneers of paying attention to how sustainable and socially responsible businesses are .   They are considered to be more affluent, educated, and ethnically & culturally diverse, practicing teamwork, modesty, search for a higher purpose and optimism .   As global connectivity soars, it is now easier to describe millennials as a distinctive cohort,  since   availability of the Internet helped to erase many socioeconomic boundaries, as people all over the world gained access to common sources of information. To understand how millennials changed the business, it is necessary to first understand what are their features as consumers and employees, since these  are the ways they had the biggest impact as stakeholders. First of all, Generation Y , in comparison with the previous generations, tend to care more for:  Diversity  and inclusion  – millennials encountered the rise of the internet and social media at a young age, so representatives with access to the global web got exposed to  other cultures and points of view more often than their predecessors. This might be related to the fact that Generation Y are more critical towards businesses’ reputation , especially in issues related to any kinds of discrimination – this generation values diversity and inclusion in their employers and businesses in general, since their youth was at the same time when antidiscrimination movements like feminism started to be more vocal about their value s .  Social responsibility  –   Millennials’ expectations from their employers and companies they are buying from are very different from  previous generations, and one of the ways this changed the business environment is that Generation Y made companies act more socially responsible. Many millennials, in comparison with their predecessors, have a need to know that the companies they work for or buy from are socially responsible, and this can include minor and major acts of service, such as helping people in need, sponsoring initiatives with a positive impact on a society, or working with local suppliers instead of big corporations. The concept of a reputation institute emerged as millennials were young, as information about businesses’ deeds started to be available easier on the Internet, and millennials, more than representatives of Generation X, want to know that companies they deal with make a positive change or at least bring no harm .  Work-life balance  –  Generation Y challenged the concept of a traditional working life by demanding more flexibility, work-life balance and comfort. It is especially evident in a case of younger millennials –  they  are not interested in burning out at work, instead they value healthy lifestyles with a balance of private life and work .  This distinctive feature of Generation Y changed many workplaces and the perception of employees by companies, as there is plenty of different opportunities for work now, and many companies started to provide a work-life balance to win over talents.  Casualty and accessibility  –  As consumers, m illennials  had a major influence on a concept of owning things and  the features of products provided to them. Two big shifts here are an emergence of a sharing economy and the casualty of luxurious segment. Millennials showed lower connectivity to material goods, and valued having an access to goods instead of owning them. This changed the consumer market, and entailed an emergence of businesses, aimed at providing goods for a short period of time :  this included usage of  online  platforms  and renting services. In terms of the luxurious   s ector ,  millennials drastically reshaped it, as they  brought a casual touch of informality and fun to many industries, and it is especially evident in the apparel and restaurants segments. Generation Y representatives are known  to   be more interested in clothes and accessories being casual and comfortable, and  many old luxurious brands had to adapt, introducing streetwear or comfortable casual clothing in general .  Collaboration   –  Coming  to the business environment  after self-reliant and  relatively self- centered Generation X, millennials are often considered to be more collaborative and social. Representatives of this generation are   quite optimistic in their perception of surroundings , including colleagues,  and  they are known for preferring team culture instead of a  competitive one. For the Generation Y, collaboration and friendly relationships with their colleagues play a big role, as they value comfortable ambiance at their workplaces. Millennials are more flexible in their perception of success,  they are not focused solemnly on material goods, but instead value experiences, their mental health and human interaction. In many countries, it was this millennial generation, that emerged the concept of a convenient and friendly workplace with a good treatment from an employer, and this reshaped the labor market in the 2000s .  Historical perspective on general shifts helps to understand that businesses and their key stakeholders did not change over a night, as it was a long process of changing values, preferences and behavior patterns. Generation X was studied  and  mostly in the West  and  Generation Y or Millennials, which  matured during the emergence of the worldwide connectivity and, thus, were researched as a global phenomenon . T hese two cohorts made their distinctive characteristics change the business environment into its current form with relatively new norms and practices. Now,  businesses’ stakeholders acquire a face of the new Generation Z or the zoomers, what entailed many changes in how businesses act, and even more changes are inevitable. Therefore, it is currently crucially important to understand global trends  related to this topic  and common features of Generation Z  from the perspectives from which they affect businesses, as this will help companies adapt  their strategies  earlier and win over new consumers and employees.  2.2. Generation Z: global trends and respective impact on the markets   As it has been discussed earlier, generations have various distinctive features, which are often caused by the fact that people born around the same age are likely to witness similar major historical events /social movements and consume similar information, especially now, as  the world is conquered by  connectivity . According to the generational theories described in the conceptual framework, the affects from witnessing the same significant events, technologies or social movements make people develop similar patterns of behavior, values, preferences, and  other common features, which affect  them  as businesses’ stakeholders.  As global connectivity expands, generational shifts are expected to play a more important role in influencing people’s patterns of behavior than socioeconomic differences do.  Global trends, beliefs and social movements  on the internet  are having  a significant i mpact  on people of all ages and socioeconomic backgrounds, and one of the   factors  under this influence  is the way people perceiv e  businesses .  As it has already been discussed earlier, businesses are under heavy influence of their stakeholders, and now the new waves of these stakeholders are Generation Z as companies’ consumers and employees. The aspects, in which businesses get most influence from this new generation, are related to consumption and employment, so this part will cover global trends related to Generation Z as businesses’ stakeholders and their respective impact on consumer and labor markets.  The findings of this part are based on the analysis of primary and secondary data , and will later be used  in identifying key differences between Generation Z consumers and employees as they are perceived with a global generalization and them in the CIS region.  An analysis and description of Generation Z from the perspective of the characteristics that have an  impact/influence business  is needed for developing proposals for strategic management of organizations willing to adapt to the new wave of their stakeholders in the CIS markets.  First, Generation Z from the perspective of being consumers, and, thus, key stakeholders of the businesses are analyzed.  Wood (2013) states that Generation Z as consumers  are likely to have  hold the following features: 1) high interest in new technologies, 2) need for products to be easy in use, 3) a need to have a feeling of safety, and 4) temporary escapism from realities the y  face . The last two characteristics can be explained by the fact that representatives of Generation Z have already encountered many political, social, technological and economic changes despite being quite young (Ernst and Young, 2015) . Generation Z, being the first digital natives and growing up with social networks and available smartphones, are significantly different from the previous generations. This generation has an innate comfort with the virtual world, as for them as consumers internet has always existed. They are not only interested in new technologies, as stated above, but they are also native with them. This fact influences the ways Generation Z perceive information  sent  to them by companies. According to the study conducted by IBM Institute for Business Value, in which 15600 zoomers from 16 countries were surveyed, around 74% of respondents chose spending time on the Internet as the most common thing for them to do when they are free. For a broader understanding, the next three activities are watching TV and movies, hanging out with friends and spending time with family, but less than 50% of respondents chose these options . In the same survey, when asked about their preferred devices, 75% of Generation Z representatives chose mobiles phones, and around 25% of the respondents admitted to spend more than five hours a day using their smartphones. The ways in which zoomers use technologies and the amount of time they spend on the Internet are shaping their advertising perception, and it is necessary for companies to adapt to these peculiarities.  Moreover, not only Generation Z consumes information almost always only from the Internet, they also prefer specific types of it. This tendency was mentioned by interviewees from Unilever, Beeline (SkyMobile) , KFC and Levi’s – most zoomers have a short span of attention, and they only pay attention to advertising that is extremely bright, colorful, and doesn’t contain long sentences or messages, but instead is able to make sense using only short phrases.  Another crucial fact about Generation Z is that they, according to studies, tend to have little to no brand loyalty and they care more about  specific  product’s features and their own experience with it. For example, Schlossberg (2016), stated that  most  zoomers make purchase decisions  only based on characteristics of products in questions, and not on their loyalty towards businesses . This makes sense, as Generation Z is called “The True Gen”, what implies that they tend to  not rely on their feelings about companies, but instead  conduct research on products’ features, ways of production,  and  retailer reputation .   Moreover, Schlossberg refers to Marcie Merriman, executive director of growth strategy and retail innovation at Ernst & Young, who told him in an interview that Generation Z expect businesses to care about their experiences and be loyal to them, but there is no brand loyalty from the consumers. As mentioned in the research, Generation Z are known to have much higher standards for products in comparison to their predecessors, and this might be caused by the fact that zoomers have always had a broader choice of goods, since they were raised as globalization has been soaring.  This global trend was mentioned by many senior marketing managers interviewed by the author of the paper. For example, according to the  senior marketing manager  of Levi’s  and a brand owner from Unilever, Generation Z are much more critical towards companies’ reputation and scandals than their predecessors, and it is known and accepted in the business environment . This can be explained by the  global web and social media prevalence, as major scandals get known by enormous number of people in a short period of time, so businesses’ reputation can be damaged if no preventive measures are taken. Both of the above-mentioned interviewed specialists mentioned that it is always discussed in their companies  that it is much easier to check in advance if company’s advertising campaigns, online activity or any other actions can be problematic than to deal with the society’s and especially online users’ outrage after an emergence of a controversial situation. Another distinctive characteristic of Generation Z is related to the attention they pay to socially important issues  and the fact that they expect companies to be good corporate citizens, which make a positive impact on a society . One of the most insightful research on Generation Z was conducted by Tracy Francis and Fernanda Hoefel (2020) from McKinsey & Company in a form of a survey among 2321 respondents and following conclusions. According to their research, 81% of Generation Z respondents agreed that they would stop buying brands and positively speak about companies whose campaigns they perceived as macho, 79% and 76% agreed they would do so if they found campaigns racist and homophobic respectively .  It has never been more important for brands to have a sense of their higher purpose and clearly project it through communication with the community. Generation Z is very vocal and proactive about trying to make the world a better, safer and more comfortable place, and it is a global tendency that their perception of companies and buying choices reflect their system of values. According to the independent MNI survey, more than half of the respondents, which were representatives of Generation Z, say that they pay a lot of attention to whether the business is dedicated to helping dealing with problematic social issues, is environmentally friendly and conscious, has strong system of values and communicates it, and projects an impact-driven image.  Zoomers try to choose companies, which are good corporate citizens, and when a business reads the room and takes a strong stance, this generation is extremely likely to come to the online community to support them, as it happened with Mattel supporting same-sex marriage. The voices of Generation Z are amplified by social and digital media, and they take a full advantage of it, by producing relevant content, advocating social issues they find important, and making sure  businesses and their marketers understand that they need to provide relevancy and value when projecting messages to this new wave of consumers .  This peculiarity of zoomers was also mentioned by the  brand owner of AXE  from Unilever  and a senior marketing manager from Levi’ during their interviews .  They both  confirmed that  it is acknowledged in their companies that zoomers  tend to pay a lot of attention to companies’ attitude towards social issues  and   them  hav ing  bigger message s  behind their products and activities . To be more specific, Generation Z is believed to  think that goods should be not only about their feasible characteristics, but they should also be somehow related to solving society’s needs, having positive impact on relevant social issues.  The authors of “True Gen: Generation Z and its implications for companies”  also point out the fact that Generation Z is considered to be more pragmatic and realistic consumers who tend to access and evaluate various information before making a purchase. They are said to analyze not only what they purchase, but also the act of consuming itself, which has gained a new meaning for this generation, as they don’t find it necessary to own something, but it is enough for them to just have an access to services and goods. Thus, value is created by accessing something unlimitedly - for example, it includes car-sharing, subscriptions, Airbnb, streaming services, and etc. Moreover, the role of sustainable development for zoomers is pointed out, as this research, as well as the previously mentioned study by Institute for Business Value has identified that a bigger number of zoomers compared to their predecessors try to buy products of companies which follow principles of sustainable development and corporate social responsibility. According the survey conducted by IBV among 15600 representatives of Generation Z, around 45% of them admitted that they choose brands which are eco-friendly and socially responsible. The limitations of this research, just as in case of the most similar studies in English, are geographical and cultural, as the respondents are from Western countries , but this tendency coincides with general global trends associated with this generation.   To support the claim, a recent study based on responses from around 2000 representatives of Generation Z found that two-thirds of this generation’s consumers experience an increase in positive feelings towards a brand  if they start to associate it with a social cause, and 58% said this association is likely to cause a purchase .  Another distinctive  characteristic  of Genera tion  Z from a global perspective is that they proved to be more pragmatic than their predecessors,  millennials. According to various sources, this can be caused by the fact that Generation Z were mostly raised during economic boom, while Generation Y grew up during a recession. Moreover, as it was described earlier, millennials hyped up concepts like freedom and experience, since they matured as the world was becoming more and more globalized, and they saw the boundaries being erased, so this made them way more optimistic and enthusiastic than generations before and after them. However,  Generation Z  were exposed to social media and other online platforms from a very young age, this affected the extent of their awareness regarding various social issues, and this made them the least go vernment and business- trusting  generation of all times. They are less idealistic and more pragmatic, and it is believed to be due to the pressure from their childhood when their parents and communities struggled with   finances and employment , so zoomers worldwide tend to be more concerned about stability, safety and pragmatic long-term investments .  As the biggest ways, companies are currently getting affected by  Generation Z becoming solvent and independent is through them being businesses’ consumers and employees, zoomers’ distinctive characteristics and patterns of behavior on the labor markets also need to be analyzed.  In this part,  global trends and tendencies associated with Generation Z as job seekers and employees are going to be discussed from the perspective of them influencing the business environment.  The distinctive features of zoomers are going to be  described with a usage of primary and secondary data, and they are also going to be  identified through  comparison with the previous generation, which is Generation Y or millennials. The comparison is necessary because it will later help to understand what aspects or practices of companies need to be changed to adapt to zoomers, and which do not. The aim of this part is to understand what factors influence the decision-making processes of Generation Z representatives when they are choosing their employers, and deciding whether they want to stay at their jobs or quit them. Identifying these aspects is crucially important now, as in 2021 oldest zoomers are 23-24 years old, what means that they have just recently entered labor markets or are doing so now. Therefore, understanding how these job seekers and employees are different from the last generation that entered the workforce 20 years ago – millennials – is important as it will play a key role in identifying how companies should adapt their strategic human resource management to be able to meet the needs and expectations of its current or future employees from Generation Z. Among other things, this thesis will be focused on  identifying both global and CIS trends and tendencies related to Generation Z as businesses stakeholders, as this is necessary for formulating  proposals for  corporate strategies of companies aiming at winning over zoomers at the CIS consumer and labor markets. An overview of the related literature  made it evident  that most of the research on intergenerational differences had Generation Y (Millennials, born 1981-1996) as a subject due to the fact its representative joined the workforce approximately twenty years ago and scholars were interested in how that new generation w as different and how it would affect businesses . A substantial body of literature about millennials, that was later referred in many of the following publications, was written by Catherine Loughlin and Julian Barling in 2001. The authors characterized millennials as young workers who are “between the ages of 15 and 24 and are a heterogeneous group, including students working part-time, evenings, weekends, and/or in the summer while attending school, and those working full time in the paid labour force and attempting to establish themselves in the adult world of work” . The authors analyzed the distinctive characteristics of Generation Y and the effect on that that had been done by the previous generations, specifically by the parents of millennials. For example, Loughlin and Barling (2001) addressed the common belief that millennials’ job expectations are supersized or unrealistic, and do not match their abilities and performance. According to the findings of the authors, many millennials shared that they were heavily affected by witnessing their parents work long hours, and then become victims of corporate downsizing, financial crises or high divorce rates which were caused by the absence of work-life balance. It is mentioned that during 1980-1990s many young millenials witnessed their parents being dismissed or downsized from companies, and that is said to root the sense of a possible betrayal (Zemke et al., 2000) . Many scholars, including Loughlin and Barling (2001), Zemke et al. (2000) and Jurkiewicz (2000) agreed on the idea that this sort of “disappointment” in employers from the young age might be the reason why Generation Y are so skeptical and not impressed by authority, and also tend to rely on themselves when they need some kind of orientation regarding their work.  Julian Barling (1999) was one of the scholars, who studied this, and he has also concluded what has been mentioned above – that millennials saw their elders make many sacrifices for with no immediate or direct payoffs, so they are much less enthusiastic about working extra hours or in general “live to work” . Hill (2002) used the term ‘‘ability-performance nexus’’ to describe the gap between what Generation Y expect to receive/achieve and what they are capable of in terms of skills and abilities . A similar finding was posed in the study of Greenberger et al.
