(2008), which found out that Millennials tend to demonstrate a sense of entitlement to good grades or high remuneration, and in most cases, these do not correspond with their actual academic or professional abilities .  And here lies one of the major similarities between Generations Y and Z,  as Generation Z are also known to be interested in instant gratification and rewards.  Moreover,  they  tend to take the most from their employer s  because they do not believe that their job is an investment and their contracts with the companies will last for a long time  considering the range of opportunities presented in the market.  Thus,  zoomers , try to maximize payoffs such as different benefits and advantages, such as flexible hours, independence, absence of strict dress-code, and having fun at work . Because of this need for immediate payoffs,  Generation Z are  perceived by the previous generations as the ones who have unrealistic goals with no corresponding experience , just as it happened with the millennials . The research conducted in those studies has very interesting practical implications, as it helped to understand the issues of motivation and work-life balance in relation to millennials.  I n terms of perceiving work and authority,  according to the interviewed managers of Unilever, Levi’s, Ololo and Beeline, Generation Z as employees are more than any other generations protective over their personal boundaries. All of the above-mentioned managers mentioned that their Gen Z colleagues would not tolerate any inappropriate or too harsh attitude neither from their colleagues, nor from their supervisors. Unlike  previous  generations, which are likely to be used to be scolded by their supervisors in a nearly rude manner, this new wave of employees  is  known to be prioritizing their mental health and personal space, so they are extremely unlikely to tolerate being treated in rude or inappropriate ways.  Specifically, the manager from Beeline mentioned that it is acknowledged in the company that younger employees, using the  generational labels  or not, will not tolerate being raised voice at and any other form of rude or extremely judgmental behavior. It has already been mentioned  earlier  that t he first wave of Generation Z entering the workforce proved to be more pragmatic and less idealistic than their predecessors. As Generation Z grew up witnessing the Great Recession and a slow decade-long recovery, as well as seeing their Generation X parents and their Generation Y siblings lose jobs, one of zoomers’ generational traits is that they are very profit and result-oriented . At the same time, zoomers value not only compensation and security aspects, but they, just like millennials, care about their work-life balance and mental health.  The below presented results of the questionnaire were acquired through surveying Generation Z representatives from the CIS countries, but they are presented in this part and not the following one, as these results fully coincide with global trends and tendencies related to zoomers.  According to the primary data of this thesis, opportunities for career growth, maintenance of work-life balance and high initial salary make top-3 priorities for zoomers when they are choosing their employers: What is most important to you in a choice of an employer? When conducting the survey, this particular question and its answer options were partly taken from the study conducted by Eddy S.W. Ng et al. (2010)   , and the research was focused on identifying issues which are considered by millenials when they graduate from universities and start seek for a job. Asking the same question among Generation Z allows to state that empirical evidence confirms that zoomers are, in fact, more focused on compensation and work-life balance than millennials. The results of the survey conducted by Eddy S.W. Ng et al. are presented below and they show that millenials chose opportunities for career growth, good colleagues and training and development opportunities as they top priorities, zoomers have aspects about T&D and good colleagues in the middle of the priorities list. This can be explained by earlier discussed theories that Generation Z is less collaborative and teamwork oriented, so they care about their colleagues less than millennials do. Moreover, zoomers perceive high initial salary and opportunities to travel to be very important factors when choosing a job, and this also confirms the above-mentioned thoughts that zoomers are more self and career-centered. The results for the study on Generation Y are presented in the table: Work-related attributes (N = 11,398, When considering employment immediately following graduation, how important are the following to your decision making?)  An interesting implication from the study is that surveyed millennials do live with some of the stereotypes that they project. For example, as it was described earlier, in the end of 1990s millennials were thought to have big ambitions and supersized goals in relation to work. And, according to the study, opportunities for advancement in position are, indeed, listed as the most important factor by the majority of the sample group. Then, previous literature addressed the fact that millenials prefer comfort and nurturing environment at work, and they are not able to sacrifice a lot as employees. This belief can be corresponded to the fact that millennials think that it is very important to be comfortable with colleagues and supervisors. Work-life balance and good variety of work have also been discussed in the literature that has already been reviewed earlier, and their position in the survey results support the idea that millennials want to enjoy work, as well as that they want to set boundaries between work and personal life, to not overwork and exhaust themselves. However, the study also illustrates that not a lot of representatives of Generation Y perceive a high initial salary to be one of the most important factors when recent graduates are looking for work. This goes against the conclusions on millennials mentioned in the analyzed publications, as many mentioned scholars had an opinion this generation tends to have unrealistically high remuneration expectations. Eddy S.W. Ng et al. (2010) said that there are two possible explanations for this mismatch between theoretical and empirical research: first, the data was collected shortly after the financial crisis emerged, so millenials could have lowered their expectations and standards because of that. Second, the sample consisted of recent graduates, and it was noticed by the researchers that students at the end of their studies tend to display lower expectations on their future salaries than they do when they are in the beginning of their academic path as bachelor students .  The fact that millenials are mentioning good people as a top priority might support the idea that they, unlike the previous generations, are interested in more than just work, but also in social aspects of it. Moreover, this might confirm another opinion on millenials – that they, unlike their parents, are not loyal to organizations, but instead to their colleagues and supervisors .   However, this is not the case for Generation Z, at least not in the CIS region, as good impression of colleagues and supervisors were chosen as one of the least influential   factors. Now, it is also important to mention that this aspect has been discussed by HR-managers from Unilever, KFC and Levi’s: they all agreed on the fact that their Generation Z colleagues did not worry a lot about their colleagues and were not enthusiastic about participating in activities aimed at strengthening the team spirit. In fact, it was pointed out by all of the three above-mentioned specialists, that zoomers  tend to  “treat work just like work, leave home and then forget about it”, implying that working takes a certain part of such young employees’ lives but not more than that. Meanwhile, previous Generations, like X and Y,  were more likely to have poor work-life balance and care about their job or colleagues on a regular basis.  According to the  various secondary data mentioned earlier, the  questionnaire distributed among representatives of Generation Z ,  and the interview with Senior   Manager s  from Unilever,  Beeline and Levi’s  zoomers  are quite pragmatic, but they prefer to  consider their mental health a top priority that is more important than remuneration or the fact that an employer can have controversial advertising campaigns: What factors are likely to make you quit your job? As it is displayed above, the top-3 things zoomers consider likely to make them leave their workplace are all somehow related to their mental health, as those are negative influence of work on mental health, discrimination and bad relationship with colleagues or supervisors. An interesting finding here is that zoomers do not consider colleagues or supervisors important when they are choosing the workplace, but do when they might decide to leave it. The theory that zoomers care for their mental health more was also confirmed by the Senior HR manager from Unilever, who stated that employees who belong to the Generation Z tend to be very focused on healthy ambiance at work. Moreover, the manager has also addressed the above-discussed fact that zoomers, unlike millennials, prefer to work individually instead of collaborating, and confirmed this tendency, while adding that despite the fact that Generation Z employees are not initiative and willing to work in teams, they do so if they are provided convenient platforms and proper guidance.  Despite the fact that millennials and zoomers are often considered to have many similar characteristics, the comparative analysis of two generations shows that they have different patterns of behavior in the labor market. One of the key differences is in the fact that Generation Z, unlike their predecessors, prefer to work on tasks alone. Millennials were much more collaborative, as many representatives found it beneficial to work in teams. However, zoomers tend to be much more individualistic and focused on individual tasks and remuneration . This  global trend  coincides with the results of the questionnaire, according to which more than 60% of respondents stated that they prefer to work by themselves: Do you prefer to work on tasks in a team or individually? Moreover,  this belief was also addressed and confirmed by the interviewed managers of Unilever, Levi’s, Beeline and KFC. All of these interviewees mentioned that they have witnessed their Generation Z colleagues be very resistant to working in teams.  For example, the HR manager of Unilever mentioned that their HR practices got reshaped because of this peculiarity, as there are many Gen Z employees at the company. She mentioned that they have designed special online platforms and created offline events aimed specifically at bonding teams consisting of younger employees. Moreover, it was said that despite the fact that Generation Z employees seem reluctant at first, they do get used to working in teams if they are provided proper guidance and support from the supervisors.  Another important characteristic of this new wave of employees is that  need transparency in everything, including business-processes, evaluation/remuneration practices and work culture. This generation is described to be very intuitive and observant, as they grew up consuming large portions of information both online and offline.  This is related to the acknowledged fact that zoomers need constant support and directions from their supervisors, as they also need they tasks and responsibilities to be very clear and transparent . This   sharply distinguishes Generation Z from millennials, as the predecessors were considered to be very independent at work, as they valued autonomy. Meanwhile, zoomers do expect to be given an opportunity to be creative and relatively free, but they also need proper and very clear directions and guidance from their supervisors . This global trend was also confirmed by all of the interviewed managers, as they mentioned that it took them a while to realize that almost all Gen Z employees won’t do any extra work or won’t go further in their projects if they are not told to.  Despite the fact that this part of the paper was originally intended to only cover global trends, information from the interviews and the questionnaire was also mentioned, as many global trends are evident in the behavior patterns and characteristics of Generation Z in the CIS region. The reason for covering zoomers from the perspective of both consumer and labor markets is that these are the ways they, as stakeholders, have the most impact on the businesses. Generation Z is in many ways similar and different in comparison to millennials, and the main changes, entailed by this generational shift is that new waves of consumers and employees expect organizations to  be more socially responsible, more proactive in solving issues negatively affecting the society, and more diverse. This generation matures in the era of social movements, aimed at erasing any types of discriminations and intolerance, and it is a global trend that zoomers tend to be less judgmental and more accepting of people who are different, so they expect the same ambiance and initiatives from businesses, be those producers and employers. As consumers, Generation Z are the first digital natives, and this made them have high tolerance from all types of online advertising, so much of the promotion just goes unnoticed. Moreover, as it was mentioned earlier, these consumers don’t tend to practice loyalty towards businesses, but they are much more likely to make a purchase if they associate a company with a good social cause.  The shift to this generation affected workplaces and HR practices in a major way, as now issues like  work-life balance, work culture transparency, non-discrimination practices, inclusion, diversity, constant supervisor support and reluctance to work in teams are as relevant as never.  According to the conducted analysis of secondary and primary data on global trends related to Generation Z, the key specificities of zoomers  as consumers  are:  A particularly keen need for a feeling of safety and affiliation to a community  Extremely short span of attention  An ability of effortlessly ignoring online advertising caused by digital nativity Little to no brand loyalty  Concern over companies’ attitude towards socially important issues Pragmatism in consumption Willingness to be aware of businesses’ stand on sustainability and corporate social responsibility And in terms of Generation Z as  job seekers and employees, the key peculiarities, tendencies and trends are:  Need for instant gratification High expectations with no corresponding experience Active protection of personal boundaries Strong concern about own mental health  Perception of work-life balance as a key priority when looking for a job Seeking flexibility at workplaces Unwillingness to work in teams A strong need to see transparency in work processes Seeking support and detailed guidelines from supervisors at every stage of working processes These analyzed global trends and tendencies help to set a ground for Generation Z specificities in the CIS region, so primary data and issues relevant for global businesses working in this region will be discussed further.  2.3. Generation Z: local CIS specificities and their impact on the markets As it became clear from the previous parts,  the commonwealth of independent states (CIS) is considered as a single market in the process of developing strategic proposals for global companies working in this region. The justification behind this decision is  based on  two main reasons – first, countries addressed as CIS have similar cultural, linguistic and socioeconomic characteristics, as they were all part of the CSSR in the past and have Russian as their official language. This leads to the people – especially younger generations – consume similar content in all of those countries, since it is mostly online and almost always in Russia, as most media and creators choose this language to access larger audience. Therefore, it is possible to make a generalization and assume that consumers, internet users and workers at the CIS markets have more or less similar behavior patterns, views, values, and trends .  Moreover,  research on differences in consuming and working habits often consider the CIS region as a whole due to many economic and social similarities .  The second reason  for analyzing Generation Z in the CIS as a whole is the fact that this analysis is done for further development of strategic recommendations or proposals for MNC working in the Commonwealth of Independent States region,  and most of the global companies have either  the same strategy for all countries (with slight appropriation) or only have offices in the biggest CIS countries (usually Russia and Kazakhstan), while considering the Russian-speaking market as a whole.  This assumption was confirmed by the collected primary data, as interviewed managers from Unilever, Beeline,  Levi’s,  KFC, and Ololo mentioned that their companies have one global strategy and value proposition, but adapt it slightly according to local cultural and social peculiarities, which are not major among the CIS countries.  Managers from  Unilever and Levi’s, for example,  specifically pointed out that their companies try to send the same message when promoting in different countries, only making changes to meet local legal specificities to not get in to problems with laws .  This part provides description and analysis of Generation Z consumers and employees in the CIS markets from the perspective of similarities and differences with the earlier discussed global trends and tendencies.  As it has been mentioned  in the previous parts,  specifically these two types of view – consumer and labor markets – are considered due to the fact that  these are the ways generational shifts have the biggest influence on businesses.  The sources of data, used in this part, inclu de previously cited and discussed publications on global trends  related to Generation Z and local CIS specificities taken from the  conducted survey among 16-23 years old representatives of Generation Z  from CIS  and Senior HR and marketing managers of MNC s  present in the region.  The goal of this part  of the paper  is to  identify and describe characteristics of this new zoomer generation  in the CIS region ,  while comparing them to global trends and looking for possible reasons should any drastic differences occur. This is done  to further   discuss what are the current challenges companies encounter while adapting to them and propose practical implications for  improvement of their strategic management.  First of all, one of the main  outputs of the previous part was the globally acknowledged belief that Generation Z are very pragmatic and more suspicious towards companies, so it is not common for them to have consumer loyalty. However, the statements regarding absence of brand loyalty does not align with the preliminary results of the survey, conducted for this thesis. One of the questions was designed to gather information on whether respondents tend to make purchases from particular companies only because of them being loyal to them or having good perception of the retailer due to previous positive experience :   Do you tend to choose retailers only because of your loyalty to the brand or previous positive experience with the company? This drastic difference between what was stated in the research conducted by Schlossberg and Ernst & Young and the findings of this paper’s primary data can be due to the life cycle of the respondents at the time of the survey. This was referred as the age or life cycle effect earlier in the conceptual framework overview. According to this effect, representatives of the same generations might show different traits and patterns of behavior depending on their age. The previously referred secondary data was collected in 2016, and it that moment the oldest representatives of Generation Z were 18-19 years old, while primary data was collected among people aged between 18-24 years, as five years have passed. Different results might occur due to the fact that people who are older are likely to be more independent and experienced consumers, as they have made more purchases than those who have just graduated from high school. Therefore, it is possible that respondents not having brand loyalty in the past was not a generational trait, but instead was a temporary phase explained by people not having a lot of independent consuming experience with brands, and, therefore, not attaining loyalty towards any of them.  Another possible explanation for this was proposed by the interviewed marketing managers of Unilever and Levi’s. According to them, young consumers in the CIS region are less  meticulous when it comes to analyzing producers and businesses in general, as they tend to be more concerned about some social issues. Therefore, while zoomers in the West  don’t tend to  have loyalty to companies based on their subjective experience, Generation Z in the CIS countries are more likely to do so, and it should affect businesses’ promotion strategies and maintenance of consumer journey.  Another  significant  difference between Generation Z globally and in the CIS region is that local zoomers are less concerned about issues related to diversity, discrimination, CSR principles and sustainability.  There is a general trend of zoomers being more aware, supportive and proactive when it comes to socially important issues, but there is still a difference between the data  from global surveys and the one conducted specifically for this thesis.    For example,  Tracy Francis and Fernanda Hoefel from McKinsey & Company  conducted a survey among  more than 2000  Gen eration  Z respondents from the US and Europe.  According to their  findings , 81% of  zoomers who responded stated  that they would stop buying brands and positively speak about companies whose campaigns they perceived as macho, 79% and 76% agreed they would do so if they found campaigns racist and homophobic respectively .  These numbers are higher than the ones obtained among 222 respondents from the CIS countries: Survey conducted among 18-24 years old Generation Z representatives The question in the survey was aimed at finding out if any kinds of scandals involving companies might make Generation Z stop purchasing their products, as previously many of the respondents showed that they consider different aspects regarding companies’ reputation and brand image when they are making a purchase or deciding to stop buying products of certain retailers. “None of the above” was listed as one of the options, however, the most common answer was quality with about 50% of the respondents choosing it, and then there are scandals related to racism, sexism and homophobia that are most likely to make Generation Z stop buying goods of any company. This peculiarity of zoomers was also mentioned by the marketing manager s  from Unilever  and Levi’s , with whom the interview was conducted. She confirmed that zoomers tend to pay a lot of attention to companies’ attitude towards social issues, and emphasized the fact that Generation Z expect businesses to have a bigger message behind their products and activities, thinking that goods should be not only about their feasible characteristics, but they should also be somehow related to solving society’s needs, having positive impact on relevant social issues.  However, the interviewed managers also mentioned that there is a major difference between younger waves of consumers as they are presented globally and as they behave in the CIS markets, because  tendencies for non-discrimination practices and awareness about sustainability are present here, but they are less prevalent in these countries.  This is especially evident in how the participants of the survey responded to the question on what factors they value the most when choosing preferred goods:    What factors are crucial in a choice of preferred goods? However, the data presented in the previous part on global trends, showed much higher positi ons  of aspects  ethical advertising, support for principles of diversity, and position of the company regarding corporate social responsibility.  The interviews with managers of  Levi’s, Beeline and Unilever coincide with this information, as all three marketing specialists mentioned that aspects like ethical advertising and diversity are becoming more and more important for the CIS youth, but zoomers are the first generation to pay a lot of attention to it, so the region is behind more developed countries in how aware and concerned young people are. However, it was mentioned by an AXE brand owner of Unilever, that their company actively conducts research on intergenerational differences in many countries, and they have  concluded  that Russia  and CIS in general do show a positive tendency and it is expected that both consumers and businesses present in this region will be much more socially active and concerned just in 5-7 years.  The top-manager from Beeline also confirmed that consumers in the CIS region are less critical and judgmental when it comes to social issues and not ethical advertising, but pointed out that their company had  a few negative experiences with Generation Z online users, as their commercial was criticized and made fun of online, predominantly by younger users who found it disturbing because of alleged sexism and deaf tone.  The Beeline manager mentioned that the company started having more negative feedback on their advertising campaigns when the social media became more prevalent, since these online platforms allow younger users, especially those belonging to Generation Z, quickly share their opinion and criticism.  Businesses and their  corporate strategies  were majorly affected by the new wave of job seekers and employees belonging to Generation Z.  Main characteristics of this generation, described earlier from the perspective of global trends and tendencies, included strong concern for  own  mental health,  unwillingness to work in teams, a need for instant remuneration and gratification, overprotectiveness of personal bo undaries , a need for constant guidance and support from supervisors, a desire to know that their work helps to make a positive impact on a society, and other factors. As it was described earlier in detail,  there are many things, which influence Generation Z in their decision-making processes regarding whether or not they want to work for a particular company. For example,  one of the general tendencies is that zoomers, in comparison with millennials, tend to pay less attention to how famous the company is, they are more worried about it being a good corporate citizen supporting important social causes.  The survey, conducted for this paper among 18-24 years old zoomers from the CIS region, supports  almost  all main global trends and tendencies known about Generation Z , but there are also some major differences. For example, one of the most globally accepted theory about this generation is that its representatives are very pragmatic and are seeking safety and stability. Therefore, they consider provided health and benefits programs to be one of the most important factors when choosing an employer. However, as it can be seen further,  this factor is the least often chosen one, and, as explained by the interviewed HR managers,  the reason behind this is that healthcare is much more affordable in the CIS region than it is in countries like the USA, South Korea, Japan, and many European countries. Therefore, Generation Z job seekers from countries of CIS do not emphasize the importance of health and benefits programs when they look for a job. Another important difference with the global trends is that surveyed zoomers seem to not perceive organization’s dedication to the CSR principles as a key factor when choosing an employer, and this can be explained by the relatively little prevalence of society’s awareness regarding such issues. Despite some of the differences between global trends on Generation Z and findings from the survey/interviews, key factors remain the same for respondents all over the world. For example,  an opportunity for maintaining  work-life balance is a top-priority for zoomers, and, as it has already been shown in the previous part, negative influence of work on one’s mental health  was chosen as the reason that is most likely to make zoomers quit their job. Also, Generation Z all over the world, including CIS, value flexibility, and this includes convenient and flexible schedule, workplace, and dress-code. The idea, mentioned by the Senior HR manager of Unilever, was that this is going to become even more common and prevalent in the closest future, as many specialists experienced remote work and might not want to come back to offices on a permanent basis.  What is most important to you in a choice of an employer? Understanding of how your target audience (either consumers or job seekers) view businesses’ responsibilities and goals is important, as it might help to form relevant value proposition and project messages that will resonate. For example, as it can be seen on a pie-chart further in the paper, despite the fact that Generation Z in the CIS region do not use the term CSR or did not choose it as an important factor in the previous question, more than a third of the respondents chose “improving society’s welfare” as something businesses have to achieve. It is also worth to point out that the respondents were asked to only choose one  factor here, and the questions did not allow multiple factors being chosen: What is the main goal that businesses have to achieve? As illustrated above,  it can be seen that despite a common point of view that  The theory of generations  cannot be applied to some regions because they are too specific, it can’t be said in relation to the countries of the CIS, as results of the questionnaire among local zoomers do coincide with global trends and tendencies. First of all, Generation Z job seekers to expect businesses to be good corporate citizens by helping the society, and this goes against common misconception that the CSR initiatives are not considered important in the CIS region. Second,  as it was mentioned in part about global trends, Generation Z have little trust towards businesses and they need things related to them be transparent and clear. Around 18% of respondents chose understandable and clear career advancement as the most important goal organizations have to achieve. Lastly, it is evident that Generation Z job seekers and employees expect businesses to provide opportunities for development of their skills (but this is the case for millennials too) and they believe that employees’ mental health should be companies’ top priority, just as it has already been discussed.  Another trend, mentioned earlier, was that  Generation Z need constant guidance and support from the supervisors, and an absence of it directly influences their desire to work at the position. A question  about that  was asked in a survey, and the results   confirmed this belief , as 63% of respondents stated they prefer to be supervised and given guidelines at every stage of the working process, 31% said they would choose to be given freedom and autonomy in completing tasks at work, and 6% decided to stay neutral on the issue. An interesting fact is that managers from Levi’s, Unilever, Ololo and KFC all said that they have noticed this pattern in their employees who were zoomers. They confirmed that almost all Generation Z specialists they met were not autonomous even if they had solid experience and knowledge, and they wanted to be guided at every step of a project completion.  A popular belief on the concept of generations affecting businesses is that countries and their cultures are too specific and unique, and it is true. However, as it was discussed earlier, global connectivity, globalization, and affordable access to the Internet let people from all over the world witness and discuss similar ideas, social movements, events, issues, and etc. This made the labels from the theory of generations more common in even non-English countries, as global companies, which originated in the West, tend to use  them. In this part, main similarities and differences between global trends and tendencies related to Generation Z and the ones common and prevalent in the CIS region, were discussed , and those are: A tendency to be loyal to brands (in contrast to the results of studies discussed in the previous part) Weaker concern about issues related to diversity, discrimination, CSR principles and sustainability Expecting organizations to perceive improvement of society’s welfare as their main goal  Work-life balance as a top priority when looking for a job Inability to maintain stable mental health as a factor most likely to make them quit the job Seeking transparency in career advancement and business-processes at work Needing constant support and guidance from the supervisors at all stages of working processes The list identifies  The se and other  characteristics of zoomers from the countries of the CIS will later be addressed in describing what companies are currently doing to adapt to them, what could be the challenges, and what strategic proposals are necessary for helping MNC adapt to the peculiarities of Generation Z in the CIS market.  Chapter  3 . Adaptation of corporate strategies to Generation Z  SPECIFICITIES   3.1. Impact of generation al  shifts on strategic management : how companies adapted in the past As it  was   mentioned  in the previous chapter,  the m ain  ways in which generational shifts ha ve had  impact on the markets and businesses is through affecting their stakeholders,  specifically consumers and employees , as they often have the most influence. It has been  discussed that  The generational theory   became much more common and globally acknowledged when globalization and global connectivity arose, so the generation that is known to have the most impact on businesses all over the world is the one that matured during the emergence of technologies and the Internet, and that is Generation Y or millennials.  The characteristics of this generation were co v ered  in detail  earlier, and, as the representatives were  born between 1981 and 1996, they started entering the consumer and labor markets around early 2000s, making businesses adapt their corporate strategies.  Just like it was explain ed and proved earlier, it was millennials who were the pioneers in many practices and concepts that are common now, including the emphasized importance of the work-life balance and mental health, flexible and convenient workplaces, non-discrimination practices in advertising campaigns, and many other factors related to businesses on consumer and labor markets.  This part provides discussion and analysis of the ways  global  companies adapted their strategies and business-processes to reach Generation Y, and it will help to  get a historical perspective on the issue of adaptation, to consider what was done earlier and what can be improved further in the paper.  As millennials started entering the consumer and labor markets as technologies and the internet soared, they were heavily influenced by them. For example, millennials are the first tech-savvy generation, and companies had to adapt their marketing strategies to reach them. One of the main strategic changes was to start being omnipresent on the Internet, as  it was in the era of millennials that  social media and all kinds of online platforms  started to be used for selling, communicating, and increasing the brand-awareness . However, it is also possible to state that companies starting to always be present on social media is a natural course of events not connected to the generational shift, and that would be fair. Nevertheless, the adaptation that was caused by millennials’ specificities was not the fact that companies started to use social media, but instead  how   they  started to do  it. As it was discussed earlier, Generation Y had little trust and much higher expectations from companies,  and emergence of online platforms, coupled with these characteristics, made companies reshape their perception of what a customer journey maintenance should look like:  Modified customer journey map For a long time, companies have been reacting to consumers, trying to place themselves somewhere in their consumer journey and anticipating their next moves, navigating consumers into the purchase. Then,  the emergence of technologies and the fact that millennials were paying attention not only to the features of a product, but also to the  overall experience during the  provided service or to the other aspects related to companies’ value chain s , consumers became as empowered as they have ever been, forcing businesses  to  change their perception  of  and approach to consumer journeys. Now, purchase or conversion into a customer is not the end of the journey, instead the most important stage comes after the goods are purchased. In the modified consumer journey , as it can  be  seen above, the final stage is called advocacy, and this is the stage on which consumers are expected to become loyal to companies and, thus, promote them among their surroundings or just on various online platforms.  This change in the stages of consumer journey is due to the fact that empowered new waves of consumers had much higher expectations than their predecessors, as the y  wanted to be aware of the producers and did not trust advertising as much as previous generations did .  It is known that more than 65% of millennials communicate with the brands online, and they were the generation that  made businesses understand how important it is to reply instantly and constantly make promotion and communication not about the company, but about the consumers. Millennial s, in comparison to  the previous generations ,   turned out more  self-centered , and the marketing strategies for this generation changed from selling to establishing the relationship and making consumers loyal. Companies started to talk not only about their products, but also about the consumers, trying to project messages that would resonate with users’ values, preferences and personal issues . These new consumers craving emotional connection with brands made companies witch towards more personalized messages, and one of the perfect examples of a company understanding millennials’ self-centricity and  need for personalization is Coca-Cola. In early 2010s, the company launched a production of personalized labels with people’s names, and it majorly boosted its sales:  “Share a Coke” campaign The campaign was based on two characteristics that were considered common for millennials – they like their stuff personalized and they  like to make things about themselves – and understanding these insights allowed Coca-Cola have one of the most successful campaigns in company’s history .  This experience is an evident example of an earlier discussed characteristics of millennials as consumers – they would reach for content that requires sharing their story, they are collaborative and like joining trends to have fun, and they embrace customized and personalized products , since they let them show their uniqueness.  Aligning brands with a cause to let millennials align with the company is another marketing strategy  that  took place after  companies started adapting to the generation of socially and environmentally aware millennials. A perfect example of such strategy is TOMS  in 2009 ,  as it  launched a campaign aimed at  lett ing its customers help  people in need, as for every sold pair of shoes another pair was sent to people from a very small village in Argentina, who didn’t have shoes.  The specialists from the company shared that millennials love to live the story and know that they and brands chosen by them contribute to a good cause, so that is the reason why that campaign was able to have a huge impact on the company and the way it’s perceived among the new generations of consumers .  The success of this initiative came with no surprise, as at that time millennials were the most socially aware and concerned generation, and various research, including the cited one, show that representatives of Generation Y are much more likely to buy from a company if they know that the businesses supports a cause they believe in, even if they would have to pay more .  Another major adaptation introduced by companies when started targeting millennials was that ads became not about trying to sell a product or service, but instead they were designed to resonate with potential customers by, for example,  speaking to and  addressing a  personal  moment in their life. Such  initiative  was introduced by  AT&T when the company undertook two steps to reach the new wave of solvent millennials: they not only went to Tumblr – a platform that was specifically popular among the generation, but also had a marketing message  that spoke directly to their memories and personal moments .  One of the main adaptations in marketing was due to the low trust Generation Y had into brands in comparison with their predecessors, and it was the strategy of nor advertising directly, but instead having partnerships with online influencers millennials already trust . This form of a friendly and subtle collaboration was more likely to help companies win over younger customers as it was proved that millennials get heavily influenced by the  purchases of their peers or people they follow on social media .  Similar idea was mentioned by the interviewed Beeline senior marketing manager, as she pointed out that ever since companies started targeting Generation Y, they would have collaborations with online influencers because this allowed to reach people who are tech-savvy and are used to skipping online advertising or ignoring it.  As it has been mentioned earlier, the main ways, in which generational shifts can influence businesses is through consumer and labor markets, as stakeholders  from there , consumers, employees and job seekers , have the most influence on companies’ adaptation of strategies when new generation becomes solvent and enters the markets. Characteristics of Generation Y or millennials discussed in the previous chapter helped to understand how different this generation was from all of their predecessors, especially in terms of their values, needs, expectations and common behavior patterns. Moreover, maturing in the era of technologies emergence and prevalence of the Internet, millennials faced global connectivity and became the first generation to  be savvy in the borderless online world, what let them adopt broader views on the world and consume  common  information  online  regardless of place of birth  and background. All these factors and those discussed earlier influenced them as businesses’ key stakeholders, employees and job seekers ,  so  companies had to adapt to that.  In the study on millennials  discussed earlier, the result of a mass survey showed that  having good people to work with and good people to report to was chosen as the second and the third important things for millennials when they are choosing future employers. This came with no surprise, as Generation Y was the first one globally to pay much attention to their mental health and to how healthy the atmosphere at the workplace is. As it was mentioned in the previous chapter, earlier generations, baby-boomers and Generation X, had different perception of the role of work and organization in their lives, but millennials grew up with less trust into business sector and they were more concerned about their own comfort and well-being.  Moreover,  technologies made it necessary for people to be able to reply to their work matters 24/7, and depression and anxiety became as common as they have ever been. It became evident for the companies that in order to keep their human resources they need to provide their employees help with conditions friendly for their mental health. One of the great examples is how in 2019 Bank of America had a 15-month training program to educate their managers about stress management and signs of anxiety/stress in employees. The senior of the company mentioned that they as an employer have a responsibility for the wellness of their colleagues and managers need to understand the programs and resources that are available. In the same year, American Express launched its first online program aimed at helping employees with their mental health, and, as mentioned by the head of HR,  and it was mentioned by the managers that they could not have expected that so many employees would use it . The fact that companies came up with various initiatives supporting employees’ mental health and nurturing a healthy environment at workplaces was majorly due to the changing expectations and needs of people on the labor market. New generation of job seekers and employees matured as the stigma regarding mental health was getting weaker and less influential, so this, along with a growing pressure from social media and a need to constantly be online for work, made millennials more vocal and open about their mental health, and, as previously cited research and companies’ strategies show, they expected their employers to support them in this issue.  One of the main ways of how workplaces changed for millennials is the amount of flexibility that became available, as many global companies tried to  help their employees maintain their work-life balance.  Workplace flexibility is related to aspects like job sharing, flexible scheduling, and remote working, and it is known to positively affect employees’  job satisfaction and productivity .  Companies, which reshaped their workplaces into making them more flexible to meet new generation’s needs and expectations are Google, GE, Johnson & Johnson, Levi’s , and many others, as 92% of millennials identify flexibility as a top priority in choosing an employer .  It was discussed earlier that Generation Y, more than their predecessors, are drawn to companies that share their values and which they respect, so another major strategic change aimed at attracting the best employees was to start supporting social causes and be vocal about it.  Despite the fact that the term corporate social responsibility was not common when millennials were entering the labor markets, the general idea of companies helping the society was already playing a big role in younger employees choosing their workplace, as this generation, as proved earlier, was very optimistic and fascinated by the idea of having a positive impact on the world .  Companies  that were early in understanding how corporate social responsibility (CSR) – related activities can enhance employer attractiveness  include  Unilever, Nestle,  L’Oréal, Bosch, Microsoft, Levi’s, Adidas and many others .  Unilever is a great example of  reshaping employer value proposition (EVP) and employer brand (EB) to meet the expectations of job seekers in a world that is highly disruptive, as they changed their EVP and EB, basing them on the power of purpose and helping their employees being catalysts for change .   Generation Y or millennials grew up in a continuously changing world of technologies and global connectivity, and it majorly influenced their needs, expectations, values and preferences in a role of businesses’ key stakeholders – consumers and employees. Understanding their key characteristics helped to explain the differences the generational shift had on companies and their strategies. This, in turn, was necessary for further analysis of how companies adapt to the last generational change, as solvent representatives of Generation Z recently started to enter consumer and labor markets. Therefore, the discussion of how businesses are currently adapting to zoomers will be provided further in the paper,  and it will be used for development of proposals for adapting corporate strategies of companies willing to work with Generation Z in the CIS region.    3.2. Corporate strategies changes in generation Z era: current trends and challenges As it has been mentioned earlier in this paper, there are many different characteristics of Generation Z that affect their patterns of behavior as consumers and employees .  To develop practical implications for adaptation of companies’ strategies to these peculiarities  in the CIS region , it is necessary to gather information on what companies are already doing  globally  to  make themselves more attractive to the new waves of stakeholders. This part is based at collected primary and secondary data, and presents information on current initiatives of companies trying to adapt to Generation Z and the challenges they face while doing so.  When discussing content that can be effective in promoting among Generation Z, one of the key features of a campaign is that it should be shareable. Zoomers are the first digital natives, and those of them who grew up with an access to the Internet, are natural storytellers. Shareable content is not only about projecting messages that users can resonate with, but it is also choosing platforms which allow people to react to campaigns and share them. As it was mentioned earlier, in case of millennials this role was taken by Tumblr, but with zoomers these share-friendly online platforms are Twitter and TikTok . While social media like Instagram and Facebook are mostly designed for posting one’s own content, Twitter is very share-friendly, as it shows on user’s account when they reply or react to a person’s or company’s post, and TikTok is business-friendly, as its distinctive feature is that the platform always have trends, and companies can participate, making their accounts viral.  It is known that social media influence around 80% of Generation Z purchases, and since zoomers value personalized content and approach, companies go to share-friendly platforms, which allow them to have a conversation with consumers . A great example of companies leveraging social media to reach  the  new wave of  zoomer  consumers is  Korean Entertainment Agencies, which made twitter their main platform for promoting new products, because it is easier to get coverage there, since whenever a user interacts with a tweet promoting anything, it will be seen in their account and reach their followers . The companies drastically changed their marketing campaigns when it became evident that with Generation Z, Twitter is much more effective as it allows to make people share your content, it helps to create a community and it is great for having a dialogue with your target audience.  The aspect about creating a loyal community brings us to the next important factor  that  companies changed trying to adapt to zoomers, and that is the message they have for their consumers.  The further discussed information about Unilever and Levi’s is based on interviews with their marketing managers, Alisa Tizik and Marina Saparova.  Unilever is  a great  example of a  company  that has  been successfully adapting to different generations of consumers for years .  The company went from having ads that were addressed as sexist and discriminatory to providing customers with motivating and encouraging messages through advertising. One  of the most noticeable change in  how the company adapted  its value proposition and message s  is with the brand AXE, which capitalized on models who were ideally shaped and translated the tagline “AXE effect”, that implied that women would be attracted to and do anything for men who use brand’s products .  However,  recently  AXE adapted its advertising campaigns to the values and expectations of millennials and zoomers, and got rid of such stereotypical promotion. Unilever started its “Find Your Magic” campaign that embraced people with physical features that would not be considered desirable according to current beauty standards. The campaign didn’t have only perfectly shaped models, but instead embraced different people’s uniqueness, encouraging them to find their own magic or what is special about themselves . To gather a first-hand information about AXE brand and its adaptation to Generation Z, I interviewed Alisa Tizik, brand owner of AXE Russia. According to the interview, Unilever is currently actively collecting data on peculiarities of Generation Z, and its marketing departments in all countries are considering these distinctive features, as they agree with the idea that consumers which are now in their early twenties are different from the previous generation of millenials in terms of their values, preferences and ways of perceiving information and advertising.  The manager agrees that the CIS market is not as active when it comes to social causes and is not as tolerant when it comes to diversity and inclusivity, but the pointed out that according to the research conducted in Unilever, major changes occurred when zoomers entered the markets, as this generation is more socially-aware.  A similar idea was mentioned by Levi’s marketing manager, who said that their company has a global strategy for all regions, and the message is aimed at resonating with younger consumers, who are now in the first wave of Gen Xers who are becoming solvent consumers. The ma nager pointed out that intergenerational differences are acknowledged in Levi’s, and the company chose to target Generation Z by creating a community with the same message in all regions and countries. Levi’s values diversity and inclusion, and they try to show it in all of their advertising campaigns. However, sometimes it can be challenging, as people can have different views depending on their socioeconomic background. For example, recently the company had an ad in Russia, and the poster had people of different races on it, what caused a h eated discussion online: Levi’s advertising for a Saint Valentine’s day   The image was in a congratulatory post of Levi’s Russia on Saint Valentine’s day, and it got many negative comments because the audience could not relate to it and believed that it was inappropriate. However, the marketing manager of the company emphasized that Levi’s goal is to build a global community, and they have the same advertising campaign for all markets. Moreover, it was mentioned that despite having negative reactions, this campaign had a positive impact, as it raised an important issue of racism in Russia with active news coverage.  Marina Saparova mentioned that it is quite challenging to project the values of diversity, non-discrimination and other social issues in the CIS markets, but Levi’s took a stand of believing that it is worth it, because there is positive dynamics and the company believes that being a pioneer now will help to gain younger consumers’ loyalty and support.  When talking about online promotion and visualization of advertising /social media posts ,  interviewed managers of Beeline, Levi’s, Ololo, KFC, and Unilever had the same opinion : First, zoomers have an extremely short attention span, and the posts need to make people interested – this can be achieved through interesting titles or messages on a picture itself. It is necessary to make the message either resonate with the person, or just very interesting, only this might make always-scrolling zoomers pay attention to your posts.  Also, it is important to make the posts and phrases as short as possible, because younger consumers are used to only reading short messages.  Second, in case of advertising, the managers mentioned that since Generation Z are digital natives, they are used to all kinds of online advertising, so they  don’t notice it unless it is extremely bright / not stative and has on it models to which the users can relate.  Third, all specialists agreed that the best way to promote to Generation Z is through online influencers or bloggers because nowadays consumers have little trust for the companies and they are much more likely to buy something if a person they consider a reliable source comments on it.  When talking how their companies adapt to Generation Z job seekers and employees, all interviewed managers except one from KFC said that in their companies HR practices are adapted for the new wave of employees. For example, Senior HR manager from Unilever said that in their company there are special online platforms aimed at helping younger employees  dive into team projects, as they acknowledge that  Generation Z representatives are often not willing to work in teams. Also, it was mentioned that zoomers are concerned about their mental health the most in comparison to previous generations, and to support its employees Unilever launched a special team aimed at proving psychological help.  One of the main challenges of working with and adapting to Generation Z was mentioned by managers of Levi’s and Ololo, as they said that zoomers at their companies are known to always need detailed guidance and supervisor support at every step of a working process.  To adapt to this peculiarity, top-managers of  Ololo  are trained  and told to provide help and advice to their employees on a constant basis, and they also practice a delegation of responsibilities in a form of small tasks. This provides clarity to employees and managers in terms who is responsible for what, and thus the expectations are also clear for both sides.  The interviewed HR managers also talked about how their companies’ employer value proposition and employer brand changed because Generation Z peculiarities.  In case of Unilever and Levi’s, the challenges were mostly related to values and beliefs, as they wanted to make sure job seekers understand what they are, because both companies want to build a friendly community with shared values. This is related to the fact that younger employees, especially Generation Z, tend to have higher job satisfactions and  productivity if they work in comfortable atmosphere and with people who do not have any negative impact on their mental health, as it was discussed in the previous chapter.  Moreover, as it has been mentioned  previously,  Generation Z values instant gratification, and this information was also confirmed by all the interviewed HR managers. To be specific, a manger from Ololo mentioned that they deliberately have meetings every morning, where all tasks for the day get written where the whole team will see them, and every time an employee is done with their task, they can come and circle it on the board. According to the managers from the company, this helps to not only structure responsibilities, but boost employees’ motivation and also feeling of gratification when they are able to show that they are done.  Little things like these help young Generation Z representatives, and as it was mentioned during an interview, “it is important to learn zoomers’ habits and needs as soon as possible, because they are already in the workforce”, and companies need to adapt their business-processes.  This part covered ways, in which companies are currently adapting to the new wave of their consumers and employees, who belong to Generation Z. As it was mentioned, global connectivity and access to shared information online made intergenerational differences crucial for businesses, as understanding their stakeholders will help them to attract consumers and win over the best employees. Companies change their corporate strategies and business-process in marketing and HR, as these are the spheres most affected by generational shifts. This part contains information on global practices and MNCs experience in the CIS region, and the next one provides proposals for adaptation of corporate strategies for international companies, willing to adapt to Generation Z peculiarities in the CIS market.   3.3. Proposals for adapting corporate strategies to Generation Z peculiarities: global companies operating  in the CIS region A it was discussed in the first chapter ,  The theory of generations  implies that people can be grouped into different generations according to their year of birth, and, as global connectivity and access to the Internet and its borderless exchange of information have been prevailing recently, intergenerational differences start ed  to have a big role in how businesses can perceive their key stakeholders. Globalization let many international companies from the West, where the  theory on generations  had been discussed and acknowledged long ago, come to the  CIS region, and this part provides proposals for global companies willing to adapt their strategies  and business-processes  to Generation Z in the Commonwealth of Independent States.  Just as it was done through the whole paper, here adaptation to Generation Z will be considered from two perspectives – zoomers as consumers and as employees, and the reason behind this is the fact, as it was discussed in detail earlier, these are the ways Generation Z representatives have the most impact on businesses as their stakeholders. The research questions, identified in the beginning, were: 1:  What are the major peculiarities of Generation Z and how do they affect representatives’ patterns of behavior in areas, which influence businesses’ strategic management?  2:  What are companies currently doing to adapt their strategies and business processes to Generation Z? 3:  What should businesses do to improve their corporate and business strategies by considering the features of Generation Z in the CIS region? The previous chapters answered the first two questions by providing information and analysis on  generational shifts from the perspective of how it influenced  businesses in the past, on characteristics of Generation Z as consumers and employees, and on what companies are currently doing globally and specifically in the CIS markets to adapt to this generation. This was done to lay the foundation for further development of strategic proposals to companies working in the CIS region. The managerial proposals discussed below are based on global practices and experience of companies in the CIS, and the sources for them were primary and secondary, discussed in detail in previous chapters.  Use sharing-friendly platforms The correct choice of social media plays a huge role in adapting marketing strategies to Generation Z,  since,  as it was mentioned earlier, around 80% of this generation’s purchases are somehow influenced by what they see or read online.  The recommendation is to invest into social media that allows to have a maximum coverage, and today these platforms are Twitter and TikTok. Twitter  opens many opportunities for businesses, as it shows one’s content not only to this account’s followers, but also to the followers of all accounts, which interact with it in any way. Even if a user doesn’t directly share your posts by retweeting them, but they like it or reply to you, all of their followers will see their reply with you being mentioned on their page. Unlike any other platforms, Twitter is quite easy in reaching a big number of people even if relatively not many react to your publications.  Moreover, as it was discussed earlier, zoomers represent the generation that has even less trust into businesses than their predecessors millennials, and they are likely to embrace authentic, honest and personalized content. Twitter allows companies to  have direct  conversation with their customers, since the search tool on the website is very helpful and it allows anyone to insert keywords and find posts mentioning them. Moreover, this is the platform that is most likely to have people’s honest reviews on their experience with a business, since the content there is filled with short posts on what people deal with on an everyday basis. Therefore, investing into promotion on Twitter will allow companies to a) make their messages reach a big number of people, b) have direct conversations with users mentioning them and c) react to reviews in a short period of time just by regularly searching relevant keyword, which could be the name of the company.  Using TikTok along with Twitter would be very helpful in promoting to Generation Z, as this platform gained extreme popularity among this audience during the lockdowns in 2020-21.  Another peculiarity of TikTok, discussed in detail earlier, is the fact that its algorithm is designed to make your videos viral if it finds audience to which they might be interesting. Therefore, the platform makes it possible to gain many views and get viral videos even if an account has just started and doesn’t yet have any followers. Moreover, TikTok is driven by constantly changing trends, so it is possible to leveraging them without investing a lot of time into creating creative content.  Another peculiarity of this platform lies in the fact that its posts are accompanied by certain sounds creators choose themselves, and it is possible to create your own sound/use your voice or take a popular sound that already has many videos on it. An opportunity to use popular sounds  helps to gain additional views and followers, as it is common for TikTok users to click on the sounds and scroll through videos with it. Moreover, zoomers, as the first generation that grew up with social media, are more than anybody else likely to be natural storytellers and content creators, so engaging them with different trends on both TikTok and Twitter can be very benefiting due to the sharing-friendly nature of these platforms.  Translate a message that resonates with the audience As it was discussed in the previous chapter, new waves of consumers represented by Generation Z are much more likely to acquire a product if they feel moved by what the company is projecting to them. The messages designed to call for action of buying proved to not be effective, as people, especially digital natives, have already seen plenty of them online. Now, the proposal is to make the messages not only  for  zoomers, but also  about  them. Basing advertising on issues that resonate with target audience’s insights has always been a winning move, but with this generation it is as relevant as never before due to the fact that zoomers stopped perceiving traditional advertising and are known to want the messages they get from companies be about themselves or their stories. Therefore, making sure your promotional campaigns contain messages, which  Generation Z can resonate with is the most effective method to make them interested in the proposition.  Resonating can include your messages emphasizing generation’s values and not just the products, because zoomers care about authenticity and are likely to ignore or reject marketing that feels not natural and pushed, so it is more effective to make your messaging reflection of potential consumers’ view of the world and values.  Offer communities  to meet zoomers’ demand for safety and affiliation As it was mentioned earlier, zoomers are so far the generation with the highest rate of depression and anxiety, and  one of the discussed causes is a big role of social media in their lives, as it triggers constant desire to compare oneself to others, and, thus, creates certain burdens for people. Moreover, the world has been changing on an enormous speed since the prevalence of globalization and technologies, and this is often referred to as the reason for the fact that this generation, unlike their predecessors, are more focused on stability and safety, then on interesting experiences. Another specificity of Generation Z that was addressed during interviews with various marketing specialists is this generation’s need for  a sense of belonging to a community or a cohort. Zoomers value identifying themselves with greater groups or causes then they can be associated with while being alone, so they seek communities. Therefore, brands, which will be able to provide zoomers with an impression that they are acquiring belongness to a certain community or a group are much more likely to  win over this new wave of consumers.  Create  meaningful  and/or snappy  content Since Gen Z’s attention span is proved to be even shorter than millenials’,  it is extremely difficult to get their attention, and for that the recommendation is to provide snappy content for advertising and meaningful content for the overall management of corporate social media accounts.  This proposal derives from the primary data in a form of interviews with multiple marketing managers from MNCs and secondary data about global practices in promoting among Generation Z.  First, being the first digital natives, zoomers are used to all kinds of online promotion, and most of it is left unnoticed by them now. Therefore, it is helpful to  make your online advertising snappy in terms of it being bright, colorful, and having models to which people in the region can relate in terms of their appearance of the ones which would be found interesting and attractive. Here, it is important to make sure that the content is snappy, but still meets the social norms and tendencies of the region, as this will help to avoid controversial situations as the one that occurred with Levi’s.  Second, the content on social media should be meaningful from the perspective of it representing the company’s point of view and stance on what is bothering its audience. Depending on the region, this could be related to all kinds of discrimination, oppression, mental health issues,  and similar issues. Having a stance on socially important problems and issues helps companies seem like they have a certain human spirit in it, and it adds authenticity, that is embraced and sought by Generation Z. However, being relatively vocal on such issues comes with risks and challenges, as it was discussed in the previous chapter. Therefore, the target audience and its values/challenges have to be researched before going for posting anything that can be considered controversial, as this will help to avoid problematic situations, damage to reputation and loss of potential customers. An example of content that should be avoided included congratulatory posts on holidays, which are not globally celebrated, but instead are only common for certain regions. For example, holidays like the Victory Day  or  Nooruz are recommended to be avoided by international companies working in the CIS, as they are considered to be controversial among Generation Z for the reasons like propaganda of militarism, certain religions or outdated traditions.  Associate your brand with socially important causes  Despite a common misconception that businesses’ stakeholders in the CIS region are less concerned about organizations’ commitment to corporate social responsibility (CSR) activities, the results of the survey conducted among Generation Z in these countries show that around 33% of respondents chose improving the welfare of the society as the main goal corporations have to achieve. Moreover, the same trend is evident globally, as it was mentioned earlier that around two thirds of surveyed Generation Z consumers stated that they are more likely to acquire a product of a certain producer if they know that the company dedicates its resources to important social issues and in general helps to improve the well-being of the community. Therefore, the importance of corporate social responsibility in the CIS region should not be underestimated, especially if the businesses is interested in succeeding among the new generation of consumers.  Build a community  working for a greater cause  As it was discussed in the previous chapter, Generation Z  job seekers globally and specifically in the CIS region seek two things: a feeling of belonging to a cohort and a feeling of having a positive impact on society, which can majorly impact their decision when looking for an employer.  As zoomers are the most socially-concerned generation so far, reshaping your employer value proposition to make it project that the company provides has a community or a cohort that works together for good social causes might be crucial in winning over the best talents on the market.  However, this should be done not only on a stage of recruitment, but it should be engraved into companies’ HR practices and business-processes. The ideas of belonging to a cohort and a community and not just working in a team would help to make zoomers more loyal. Talking about  personal relationship, having conflicts with colleagues and feeling that work has a bad influence on one’s mental health were chose as the top reasons for CIS zoomers to quit their jobs. Therefore, the recommendation is to  use questionnaires to  check on employees’ mental health and their  perception of their relationship with the colleagues on a regular basis. This preventive measure is expected to help prevent problems with employee turnover, as it is evident that the new wave of Generation Z workers  is majorly concerned about their mental, while they also seek a sense of belonging to a healthy cohort. It is also crucially important to make sure employees understand how the organization has a positive impact on society’s welfare, as, according to the conducted survey, it turned out that around 33% of Generation Z representatives believe that this is the main goal for businesses. Proving a sense of purpose through trainings and helping employees see how company’s projects are directly or indirectly influencing people’s lives or needs for the better are likely to affect a desire of Gen Z employees to stay or quit a job.  Provide instant gratification  and continuous supervisor support   Research on Generation Z characteristics, interviews with managers from international companies operating in the CIS region and  survey among zoomers from these countries made it clear that  most representatives of this Generation Z are relatively impatient, seek instant gratification from work and operate more effectively if they are provided supervisor support in a form of clear guidelines and directions provided at every stage of a working process.  To  help these two issues, the  practices implied in companies, which acknowledge intergenerational differences, include dividing  projects into as small tasks as possible, since this helps to make the responsibilities clear and transparently evaluate every stage, while also providing employees with an opportunity to have their needed instant gratification that would not be achieved if the tasks were larger, since the feeling of getting something done would appear much later. Moreover, the proposal to adapt to the new generation of employees by crumbling projects into as small responsibilities as possible helps to solve another issue with zoomers, as the conducted survey and secondary data show that this generation appears to seek constant support from the supervisors in a form of very clear and detailed guidelines for every stage of the process. As it has been discussed earlier, a feeling of having continuous supervisors’ support directly influences zoomers’ decision to keep the job, and having smaller tasks helps to get more clear and detailed guidelines for bigger number of steps in a working process.  Around two thirds of surveyed zoomers in the CIS region stated that they would prefer having guidance and support of the supervisors instead of being autonomous and independent in working on projects, and this was confirmed in the interviews. Therefore, making sure the projects and responsibilities are  divided into as small parts as possible helps to kill two birds with one shot – letting younger employees have their sought instant gratification and making sure those who need it have clear guidelines and  directions are more stages of the working processes.  Respect employees’ work-life balance and personal boundaries  Despite the fact that this  recommendation might seem obvious, the importance of work-life balance is still quite underestimated in companies operating in the CIS region.  Nevertheless, the survey conducted for this paper, indicated that an opportunity for successfully maintain work-life balance is the most important factor for Generation Z job seekers, while having work negatively impact their mental health (and this is often caused by the lack of the above-mentioned balance) is an issue that is most likely among all other factors make zoomers quit their jobs.  The vanishing stigma of mental health and the fact that zoomers are the generation with the highest recorded anxiety and depression majorly influence younger employees’ preferences and patterns of behavior at work. Along with helping employees to maintain work-life balance by providing realistic and manageable workload it is also important to keep personal boundaries, by  keeping criticism within the range of work-related aspects, as discrimination at work and bad relationships with colleagues/supervisors were in top reasons making zoomers want to find another workplace.  The proposals, presented above, are related to businesses’ strategies and business-process, and they were all based at global practices on Generation Z as businesses’ stakeholders, and collected primary data in a form of interviews and a survey about peculiarities of zoomers as consumers and employees in the CIS markets.  Conclusion The Theory of generations   implies that people born around the same time develop similar values, preferences, patterns of behavior and characteristics due to witnessing similar historical events, social movements and changes as they are maturing. Originally formulated in early 1920s, it only became acknowledged globally relatively recently, when globalization, prevalence of technologies and the Internet, and global connectivity soared. The theory implies certain labels for currently active generations, and those are baby-boomers (born 1946-1964), Generation X (1965-1980), Generation Y (1981-1996), Generation Z (1997-2012). Despite the labels and the concept of generation being only common in the Western countries for a long time, they are currently used all over the world, and it is  assumed that intergenerational differences are starting to play a crucial role now, as globalization and technologies are soaring, and people are now present in a borderless online world.  The goal of this paper was to develop strategic proposals for international companies, working in the CIS region and willing to adapt their strategies and business-process to Generation Z as their stakeholders.  The representatives of the generation were analyzed as consumers and employees, as these are ways, in which zoomers have the most impact on businesses.
